examples.context: Client Bree is alert. Client Elena is disciplined. Client Phoenix is punctual. If a client is diligent, then that client is capable, so any diligent person would also be capable. Whenever a client is confident, that client is astute; in particular, if Elena is confident, Elena is astute. Elena being resourceful guarantees that she is confident. Being disciplined makes Elena resourceful. If a client is systematic, then that client is logical; thus a systematic client would also be logical. Alertness leads to perceptiveness, so because Bree is alert, Bree is perceptive. Punctuality leads to proactivity, so because Phoenix is punctual, Phoenix is proactive. Either Elena is not astute, or Elena is fair‑minded. Either Bree is not perceptive, or Bree is diligent. Either Phoenix is not proactive, or Phoenix is systematic.
examples.question: Is client Elena diplomatic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether client Elena possesses the diplomatic trait. The knowledge base contains several facts about Elena's discipline and a chain of conditionals linking discipline to resourcefulness, confidence, and astuteness, as well as a disjunction that involves astuteness and fairness. Our goal is to see if diplomatic can be derived, directly or indirectly, from these premises.

I need to determine whether Client_elena is diplomatic can be derived from the given premises.

<step n="1" type="derived">

</step>

Answer: Unknown

examples.context: In the garden, the rose enjoys balanced soil. The lavender is neatly arranged, and the tomato reaches its timely harvest. Whenever a plant is neatly arranged, it is also well watered, so the lavender is well watered. If a plant is pollinator friendly, then it is pruned; thus any pollinator‑friendly tomato would be pruned. Whenever a plant is vibrant, its soil is ready, so a vibrant lavender would have soil ready. A balanced‑soil rose is also fruitful, meaning the rose bears fruit. If a rose is perennial, then it is high yielding, so a perennial rose would be high yield. A sun‑loving tomato cannot be timely harvested, i.e., if the tomato is sun loving then it is not timely harvest. Either the tomato is sun loving or it is pollinator friendly. Either the lavender is not well watered or it is vibrant. Either the rose is not fruitful or it is perennial.
examples.question: Is the tomato not pruned?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the tomato fails to be pruned. The garden facts and rules give us information about timeliness, sun‑loving, pollinator friendliness, and pruning. By chaining these premises we can see what must hold for the tomato.

The rule linking pollinator friendliness to pruning tells us that if we can establish the tomato is pollinator‑friendly, we will immediately get pruning. This connection will be crucial later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any pollinator‑friendly tomato would be pruned.</text>
<logic>pruned(tomato) :- pollinator_friendly(tomato)</logic>
</step>

The rule about sun‑loving leading to a lack of timely harvest gives us a way to negate sun‑loving once we know the tomato is timely harvest. It creates a conflict that we can exploit.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the tomato is sun‑loving then it cannot be a timely harvest.</text>
<logic>~timely_harvest(tomato) :- sun_loving(tomato)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possible ways the tomato can satisfy the garden conditions: either it loves the sun or it attracts pollinators. One of these must be true, so ruling one out will force the other.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>We are told that either the tomato is sun‑loving or it is pollinator‑friendly.</text>
<logic>sun_loving(tomato) v pollinator_friendly(tomato)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the tomato is a timely harvest is a concrete observation we can use together with the sun‑loving rule to eliminate the sun‑loving option.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The tomato is known to be timely harvest.</text>
<logic>timely_harvest(tomato)</logic>
</step>

Because the tomato is a timely harvest, the rule that sun‑loving implies not timely harvest cannot hold; otherwise we would have both timely and not timely harvest, which is impossible. Therefore we infer that the tomato is not sun‑loving. This step removes one side of the disjunction.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From the fact that the tomato is timely harvest and the rule linking sun‑loving to not timely harvest, we infer that the tomato cannot be sun‑loving.</text>
<logic>~sun_loving(tomato)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that the tomato is not sun‑loving, the disjunction "sun‑loving or pollinator‑friendly" forces the remaining alternative to be true. Hence the tomato must be pollinator‑friendly. This deduction follows directly from the exclusive nature of the disjunction.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Since the tomato is not sun‑loving and we have the disjunction that it is either sun‑loving or pollinator‑friendly, we conclude that the tomato must be pollinator‑friendly.</text>
<logic>pollinator_friendly(tomato)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the tomato is pollinator‑friendly, we can apply the earlier rule that pollinator‑friendly plants are pruned. Consequently, the tomato is pruned. This links the derived pollinator status to the desired pruning property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Given that the tomato is pollinator‑friendly and the rule that pollinator‑friendly implies pruned, we derive that the tomato is pruned.</text>
<logic>pruned(tomato)</logic>
</step>

We derived Tomato is pruned, so the conclusion Tomato is not pruned is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the chess‑strategy world, the piece called cedar is known to be courageous. Any piece that is thorough is also wise. Moreover, whenever a piece is wise it cannot be courageous. These are the only facts and rules that apply to cedar.
examples.question: Is the cedar thorough?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the cedar possesses the property of being thorough. To answer, we must combine the supplied fact about courage with the two conditional rules that connect thoroughness, wisdom, and courage.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if the cedar is thorough then the cedar is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(cedar) :- thorough(cedar)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if the cedar is wise then the cedar is not courageous.</text>
<logic>~courageous(cedar) :- wise(cedar)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the cedar is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(cedar)</logic>
</step>

We start from the fact that the cedar is courageous. The second rule tells us that any wise cedar would be not courageous. Since the cedar is indeed courageous, it cannot satisfy the condition of being wise; otherwise we would have a conflict. This is an application of modus tollens: from "wise → not courageous" and "courageous", we infer "not wise".
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because the cedar is courageous and the rule that wise implies not courageous, we infer that the cedar cannot be wise.</text>
<logic>~wise(cedar)</logic>
</step>

Now we have established that the cedar is not wise. The first rule links thoroughness to wisdom by stating that thoroughness implies wisdom. Using modus tollens again, if the cedar were thorough, it would have to be wise, but we already know it is not wise. Therefore the cedar cannot be thorough.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Given that the cedar is not wise and the rule that thorough implies wise, we deduce that the cedar cannot be thorough.</text>
<logic>~thorough(cedar)</logic>
</step>

We derived Cedar is not thorough, so the conclusion Cedar is thorough is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the runway collection, the silk scarf is described as sustainable. Designers follow a principle that any sustainable garment is also trendsetting. Moreover, they hold that every trendsetting piece automatically qualifies as stylish. Conversely, they maintain that if a piece is experimental, it cannot be stylish. These statements together constitute all the information about the silk scarf.
examples.question: Is the silk scarf experimental?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the silk scarf possesses the experimental quality. To answer this, we must follow the chain of design principles that relate sustainability, trendsetting, stylishness, and experimental status.

The first principle connects sustainability to trendsetting, so any garment labeled sustainable must also be trendsetting. This will let us move from the known fact about the scarf to a new property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if a piece is sustainable, then it is trendsetting.</text>
<logic>trendsetting(silk_scarf) :- sustainable(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

The second principle tells us that trendsetting automatically brings stylishness. It creates a bridge from trendsetting to another attribute we can later use in reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any trendsetting piece is also stylish.</text>
<logic>stylish(silk_scarf) :- trendsetting(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

The third principle states that experimental pieces cannot be stylish. This provides a way to rule out experimental status once we know a piece is stylish.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if a piece is experimental, then it cannot be stylish.</text>
<logic>~stylish(silk_scarf) :- experimental(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that the silk scarf is sustainable, giving us a concrete starting point for the inference chain.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The silk scarf is sustainable.</text>
<logic>sustainable(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

Because the scarf is sustainable and the first principle says sustainability implies trendsetting, we can confidently assert that the scarf is trendsetting. This step translates the general rule into a specific fact about the scarf.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the silk scarf being sustainable and the rule linking sustainability to trendsetting, we infer that the silk scarf is trendsetting.</text>
<logic>trendsetting(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the scarf is trendsetting, the second principle lets us infer that it is also stylish. This follows directly by applying the rule to the newly derived trendsetting property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Given that the silk scarf is trendsetting and the rule that trendsetting implies stylish, we conclude the silk scarf is stylish.</text>
<logic>stylish(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

We now know the scarf is stylish. The third principle tells us that any experimental garment would not be stylish, so the presence of stylishness forces the conclusion that the scarf cannot be experimental. This is a contrapositive use of the rule, yielding a negation of experimental.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since the silk scarf is stylish and the rule that experimental pieces are not stylish, we infer that the silk scarf cannot be experimental.</text>
<logic>~experimental(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

We derived Silk_scarf is not experimental, so the conclusion Silk_scarf is experimental is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The router has a firewall. Either the router does not have a firewall, or it supports IPv6. Whenever a router supports IPv6, it runs security updates. Any router that runs security updates also monitors traffic.
examples.question: Does the router monitor traffic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the router monitors traffic. The target property corresponds to the observant predicate, so we must show that the router satisfies the condition that leads to being a traffic monitor.

The first rule connects running security updates with monitoring traffic. Recognizing this link is essential because it tells us what we must ultimately prove about the router.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any router running security updates also monitors traffic.</text>
<logic>monitors_traffic(router) :- runs_security_updates(router)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links supporting IPv6 to running security updates. This provides a pathway from a more basic capability (IPv6 support) to the desired security‑update state.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a router supports IPv6, then it runs security updates.</text>
<logic>runs_security_updates(router) :- supports_ipv6(router)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that at least one of two statements holds: either the router lacks a firewall or it supports IPv6. This will allow us to eliminate the first alternative using the known firewall fact.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The knowledge base includes the statement that either the router does not have a firewall, or it supports IPv6.</text>
<logic>~has_firewall(router) v supports_ipv6(router)</logic>
</step>

We are told explicitly that the router has a firewall. This fact will interact with the disjunction to force the other alternative to be true.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We are given that the router has a firewall.</text>
<logic>has_firewall(router)</logic>
</step>

Because the router definitely has a firewall, the clause "the router does not have a firewall" is false. The disjunction therefore requires the remaining option—supporting IPv6—to be true, giving us logical support for the router.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>From the firewall fact and the disjunction, we infer that the router must support IPv6.</text>
<logic>supports_ipv6(router)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the router supports IPv6, we can apply the second rule which says IPv6 support guarantees that the router runs security updates. This step moves us closer to the condition needed for traffic monitoring.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since the router supports IPv6, the rule about IPv6 implies it runs security updates.</text>
<logic>runs_security_updates(router)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the router runs security updates, the first rule tells us that any such router also monitors traffic. Hence, we can conclude the router is a traffic monitor.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Having security updates means, by the first rule, the router monitors traffic.</text>
<logic>monitors_traffic(router)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Router is monitors traffic, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the laboratory, the compound named molecule_hope has been thoroughly examined, so it is well_studied. Chemists know that any compound that is chromophoric is also reactive. examples.question: Is molecule_hope reactive?
examples.completion:

We are asked to determine whether molecule_hope possesses the reactive property. The goal is to combine the given facts and logical relationships to reach a conclusion about its reactivity.

The conditional rule connects chromophoric status to reactivity, so knowing the molecule is chromophoric would immediately give us reactivity. This premise will be crucial once we can establish the chromophoric property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if molecule_hope is chromophoric, then it must be reactive.</text>
<logic>reactive(molecule_hope) :- chromophoric(molecule_hope)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either the molecule is not well_studied or it is chromophoric. Because we already have information about its well‑studied status, this statement will help us eliminate one alternative.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction tells us that molecule_hope is either not well_studied or it is chromophoric.</text>
<logic>~well_studied(molecule_hope) v chromophoric(molecule_hope)</logic>
</step>

The fact that molecule_hope is well_studied directly contradicts the first option of the disjunction. Recognizing this contradiction is essential for applying disjunctive reasoning in the next step.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given that molecule_hope is well_studied.</text>
<logic>well_studied(molecule_hope)</logic>
</step>

Given that molecule_hope is well_studied, the negation of well_studied is false, so the disjunction "not well_studied or chromophoric" can only be true if the second term, chromophoric, holds. Thus we deduce that molecule_hope is chromophoric, satisfying the disjunctive statement.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Since molecule_hope is well_studied, the first part of the disjunction (~well_studied) is false, so the only way the disjunction can hold is if molecule_hope is chromophoric; thus we infer that it is chromophoric.</text>
<logic>chromophoric(molecule_hope)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established molecule_hope is chromophoric, we can invoke the earlier conditional rule: any chromophoric molecule is reactive. Consequently, molecule_hope must be reactive.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Having established that molecule_hope is chromophoric, the conditional rule lets us infer that it is reactive.</text>
<logic>reactive(molecule_hope)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Molecule_hope is reactive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Ivan is adaptable. Max shows empathy, so he is empathetic. Rhea is fit. Whenever someone is adaptable, they are also anxious, so Ivan is anxious. Whenever someone is empathetic, they become energetic; therefore Max is energetic. If someone is fit, they are honest, which makes Rhea honest. Disciplined individuals are judged as judicious, so if Rhea were disciplined she would be judicious. Relaxed people are meticulous, thus if Ivan were relaxed he would be meticulous. Energetic beings are supportive; consequently any energetic person, such as Max, is supportive. Regarding the uncertainty, either Ivan is not anxious or Ivan is relaxed. Likewise, either Rhea is not honest or Rhea is disciplined. In the default reasoning, normally if Max is decisive and there is no abnormality preventing agility, then Max is agile. Also, normally if Max is supportive and there is no abnormality preventing decisiveness, then Max is decisive.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Max is agile?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Max can be regarded as agile. The knowledge base provides facts about Max's empathy and several conditional and default rules linking empathy, energy, support, decisiveness, and agility. Our goal is to chain these relationships to reach a conclusion about Max's agility.

The first premise tells us that empathy guarantees energy. This is crucial because it connects Max's known empathetic property to a later rule about support. Recognizing this link helps us move from a simple fact to a more useful intermediate property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any empathetic individual becomes energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(max) :- empathetic(max)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links energy to support. Once we have established that Max is energetic, this rule will allow us to infer that Max is supportive, which is a prerequisite for the default rule concerning decisiveness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any energetic individual is also supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(max) :- energetic(max)</logic>
</step>

The third premise is a default rule describing how agility can be concluded. It says that if Max is decisive and there is no abnormality preventing agility, then Max becomes agile. This rule will be applied after we manage to prove Max's decisiveness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>A default rule asserts that if Max is decisive and there is no abnormality blocking agility, then Max is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(max) :- decisive(max), not ab_agile(max)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise is another default rule that provides a way to obtain decisiveness. It states that if Max is supportive and there is no abnormality blocking decisiveness, then Max is decisive. This connects directly to the supportive state we aim to derive.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Another default rule says that if Max is supportive and there is no abnormality blocking decisiveness, then Max is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(max) :- supportive(max), not ab_decisive(max)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Max is empathetic. This fact is the starting point for the chain of reasoning, as it activates the first rule about energetic.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Max is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(max)</logic>
</step>

Having Max's empathy and the rule that empathy implies energy, we can directly infer that Max is energetic. This follows the standard modus ponens pattern: the antecedent (empathetic) holds, so the consequent (energetic) must hold as well.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Max being empathetic and the rule that empathetic implies energetic, we infer that Max is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(max)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Max is energetic, we can apply the second rule, which says energetic individuals are supportive. By modus ponens again, Max's energetic status leads to the conclusion that he is supportive.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Using Max's newly derived energetic status and the rule that energetic implies supportive, we conclude Max is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(max)</logic>
</step>

With Max established as supportive, we can trigger the default rule that yields decisiveness, assuming no abnormality blocks it. Once decisiveness is obtained, the second default rule about agility kicks in, again assuming no abnormality prevents agility. Combining these two default inferences gives us Max's agility.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4,3">
<text>Applying the two default rules together—supportive leads to decisive (absent abnormality), and decisive leads to agile (absent abnormality)—we derive that Max is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(max)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Max is agile, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The eighth rule indicates that a judicious person is proficient, which will later allow us to infer consistency and then bravery.

We are given explicitly that Emma is clear-headed, providing the factual basis for applying the diplomatic rule.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Emma is clear-headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(emma)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Emma is clear-headed together with the rule that clear-headedness yields diplomacy, we conclude Emma is diplomatic. This is the first derived property beyond the initial fact.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>From Emma being clear-headed we infer that Emma is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(emma)</logic>
</step>

With Emma now known to be diplomatic, the rule that diplomatic persons are focused lets us infer that Emma is focused. This moves the chain forward one more link.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Since Emma is diplomatic, we infer that Emma is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(emma)</logic>
</step>

Since Emma is focused, the rule that focused individuals are judicious allows us to assert Emma is judicious. This prepares us to reach proficiency.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,7">
<text>Because Emma is focused, we infer that Emma is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(emma)</logic>
</step>

Given Emma's judiciousness, the rule that judicious people are proficient lets us infer Emma is proficient. This is a crucial milestone because proficiency will later give us consistency.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,8">

examples.context: Patient Faith is anxious. Patient Hazel is vigorous. Whenever a patient is scholarly, that patient is alert. If a patient is not enthusiastic, then that patient is brilliant. Whenever a patient is anxious, that patient is precise. If a patient is enthusiastic, then that patient is not vigorous. Regarding Patient Faith, either the patient is not precise or the patient is scholarly (or both).
examples.question: Based on the information given, is patient_hazel brilliant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether patient_hazel is brilliant given a set of facts and conditional statements. The goal is to chain the available information to reach a conclusion about her brilliance.

This premise provides the direct connection we need: whenever patient_hazel lacks enthusiasm, she will be brilliant. Keeping this in mind will allow us to derive brilliance once we can show she is not enthusiastic. It is a crucial bridge between the property we want to prove and a condition we can potentially establish.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if patient_hazel is not enthusiastic, then patient_hazel is brilliant.</text>
<logic>is_brilliant(patient_hazel) :- ~is_enthusiastic(patient_hazel)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that if patient_hazel were enthusiastic, she would not be vigorous. This conditional will be used in reverse to argue about her enthusiasm based on what we already know about her vigor. It sets up a relationship that can be exploited via a contrapositive reasoning step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if patient_hazel is enthusiastic, then patient_hazel is not vigorous.</text>
<logic>~is_vigorous(patient_hazel) :- is_enthusiastic(patient_hazel)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a concrete fact: patient_hazel is vigorous. This positive statement about her vigor will interact with the previous conditional to let us infer something about her enthusiasm. It provides the factual anchor needed for the next logical move.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that patient_hazel is vigorous.</text>
<logic>is_vigorous(patient_hazel)</logic>
</step>

Because patient_hazel is vigorous, the antecedent of the rule "if enthusiastic then not vigorous" cannot hold; otherwise she would be not vigorous, contradicting the fact. By applying Modus Tollens, we conclude that patient_hazel is not enthusiastic. This step flips the conditional, turning a known consequence into a denial of its cause.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>From patient_hazel being vigorous and the rule that enthusiasm would make her not vigorous, we infer that patient_hazel cannot be enthusiastic; therefore she is not enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>~is_enthusiastic(patient_hazel)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established patient_hazel is not enthusiastic, we can invoke the first rule directly: lack of enthusiasm guarantees brilliance. Using Modus Ponens, the condition is satisfied, and we derive that patient_hazel is brilliant. This step completes the logical chain from the initial facts to the desired property.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since we have established that patient_hazel is not enthusiastic, the rule linking lack of enthusiasm to brilliance lets us conclude that patient_hazel is brilliant.</text>
<logic>is_brilliant(patient_hazel)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Patient_hazel is is brilliant, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Cora is known to be knowledgeable. Whenever something is knowledgeable, it is also adaptable, so Cora being knowledgeable makes her adaptable. If someone is studious, then that person is energetic; likewise, any energetic individual is observant, and any observant individual is trustworthy. In addition, either Cora is not adaptable or Cora is studious. These statements capture all the facts, conditionals, and the exclusive alternative that we will use to reason about Cora's trustworthiness.
examples.question: Is Cora trustworthy?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Cora can be considered trustworthy based on the information provided. The goal is to chain together the given facts and conditional statements until we reach a conclusion about her trustworthiness.

The first conditional tells us that knowledge implies adaptability. Recognizing this link is crucial because it connects the known fact about Cora's knowledge to a property we will later combine with other premises.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Cora is knowledgeable, then Cora is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(cora) :- knowledgeable(cora)</logic>
</step>

The second rule establishes a bridge from being studious to being energetic. This will become important once we can show that Cora is studious, allowing us to move further along the chain.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Cora is studious, then Cora is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(cora) :- studious(cora)</logic>
</step>

The third conditional states that energetic individuals are observant. This step will let us translate any derived energetic status into observance, which is one more link toward trustworthiness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Cora is energetic, then Cora is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(cora) :- energetic(cora)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule asserts that observance guarantees trustworthiness. This is the final conditional we need: once we know Cora is observant, we can directly conclude she is trustworthy.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Cora is observant, then Cora is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(cora) :- observant(cora)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either Cora is not adaptable or she is studious. This logical choice will help us infer studiousness once we have information about adaptability.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Cora is not adaptable, or Cora is studious.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(cora) v studious(cora)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Cora is knowledgeable. This fact is the starting point for the entire deduction, as it activates the first conditional about adaptability.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Cora is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(cora)</logic>
</step>

Because Cora is knowledgeable and we know that knowledge leads to adaptability, we can safely assert that Cora is adaptable. This derived fact will interact with the disjunction in the next step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Cora being knowledgeable and the first rule, we infer that Cora is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(cora)</logic>
</step>

We now have Cora as adaptable, which makes the first part of the disjunction (~adaptable) false. To keep the disjunction true, the second option must hold, so Cora must be studious. This deduction turns the abstract alternative into a concrete property.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Using the fact that Cora is adaptable together with the disjunction, we deduce that Cora must be studious.</text>
<logic>studious(cora)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Cora is studious, we can apply the second rule, which tells us that any studious individual is energetic. Thus, Cora inherits the energetic property, moving us further along the chain toward observance.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given that Cora is studious and the second rule, we conclude that Cora is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(cora)</logic>
</step>

With Cora now known to be energetic, the third conditional allows us to infer that she is observant. This step translates the energetic status into the observant quality required for the final implication.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Since Cora is energetic and the third rule applies, we obtain that Cora is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(cora)</logic>
</step>

Since Cora is observant, the fourth rule directly yields that she is trustworthy. This completes the logical progression from the original fact to the desired conclusion.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>From Cora being observant and the fourth rule, we derive that Cora is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(cora)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Cora is trustworthy, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the garden, the rosemary plant is thriving. The thyme plant has pointed leaves. Whenever a rosemary is soil-rich, it becomes hardy. Regarding thyme, either it is not organized or it is watchful.
examples.question: Is the rosemary plant hardy?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the rosemary plant possesses the hardy quality. To answer, we must trace the logical connections among the garden facts and rules that involve rosemary’s soil richness, seasoning, and thriving status.

The first rule tells us that soil richness guarantees hardiness for rosemary. This conditional will be crucial later when we establish that rosemary is soil-rich.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If rosemary is soil-rich, then rosemary is hardy.</text>
<logic>hardy(rosemary) :- soil_rich(rosemary)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links seasoning to a lack of thriving. It sets up a conflict: if rosemary were seasoned, it could not be thriving, which we already know to be true.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If rosemary is seasoned, then rosemary is not thriving.</text>
<logic>~thriving(rosemary) :- seasoned(rosemary)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities for rosemary: it is either seasoned or soil-rich. One of these must hold, but we do not yet know which.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Rosemary is either seasoned or soil-rich.</text>
<logic>seasoned(rosemary) v soil_rich(rosemary)</logic>
</step>

The fact that rosemary is thriving is directly stated in the garden description, providing a concrete truth to work with in the upcoming inference steps.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Rosemary is thriving.</text>
<logic>thriving(rosemary)</logic>
</step>

We combine the fact that rosemary is thriving with the rule that seasoning would prevent thriving. Since thriving is observed, the antecedent (seasoned) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Thus we conclude rosemary is not seasoned.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Because rosemary is thriving and being seasoned would make it not thriving, rosemary cannot be seasoned.</text>
<logic>~seasoned(rosemary)</logic>
</step>

Having ruled out the seasoned option, the disjunction from premise 3 forces the other alternative to be true. Therefore, rosemary must be soil-rich.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Since rosemary is not seasoned, the remaining alternative is that it is soil-rich.</text>
<logic>soil_rich(rosemary)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established rosemary is soil-rich, we can apply the conditional from premise 1. Soil richness directly leads to hardiness, so rosemary is indeed hardy.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Given that rosemary is soil-rich, it follows that rosemary is hardy.</text>
<logic>hardy(rosemary)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Rosemary is hardy, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the study of rock formations, quartz, basalt, shale, and limestone each display certain characteristics. Quartz is thermally active and also structurally complex. Basalt is thermally active and also structurally complex. Shale is structurally complex. Limestone is thermally active and also structurally complex. Whenever a rock is structurally complex, it is also high permeability. Whenever a rock has high permeability, it becomes thermally active. Whenever a rock is thermally active, it is chemically sensitive.
examples.question: Is the shale chemically sensitive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the shale possesses the property of being chemically sensitive. To answer, we must follow the chain of geological rules that connect structural complexity, permeability, thermal activity, and chemical sensitivity.

The first rule links structural complexity to high permeability for any rock. Recognizing this connection is essential because we know shale is structurally complex, so the rule will allow us to assign permeability to shale later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The geological rule states that any structurally complex rock is also high permeability.</text>
<logic>high_permeability(shale) :- structurally_complex(shale)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that high permeability guarantees thermal activity. This bridge will let us move from a permeability conclusion to a thermal activity conclusion once we have established permeability for shale.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The geological rule states that any rock with high permeability becomes thermally active.</text>
<logic>thermally_active(shale) :- high_permeability(shale)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects thermal activity to chemical sensitivity. It provides the final step needed to reach the query's property, assuming we can show shale is thermally active.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The geological rule states that any thermally active rock is chemically sensitive.</text>
<logic>chemically_sensitive(shale) :- thermally_active(shale)</logic>
</step>

The fact that shale is structurally complex gives us a starting point for the chain of implications. Since the first rule applies to any structurally complex rock, this fact will trigger the derivation of permeability for shale.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Shale is known to be structurally complex.</text>
<logic>structurally_complex(shale)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that shale is structurally complex with the first rule, we deduce that shale must be high permeability. This follows directly because the rule states that structural complexity implies permeability for all rocks, and shale satisfies the antecedent.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From shale being structurally complex and the first rule, we infer that shale is high permeability.</text>
<logic>high_permeability(shale)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established shale is high permeability, we apply the second rule, which says high permeability leads to thermal activity. Consequently, shale becomes thermally active, moving us one step closer to the desired property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From shale being high permeability and the second rule, we infer that shale is thermally active.</text>
<logic>thermally_active(shale)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that shale is thermally active, we invoke the third rule that any thermally active rock is chemically sensitive. This yields the conclusion that shale is chemically sensitive, directly answering the query.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>From shale being thermally active and the third rule, we infer that shale is chemically sensitive.</text>
<logic>chemically_sensitive(shale)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Shale is chemically sensitive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Park lynx is known to be neutral. Zion eagle is energetic. In this reserve, any animal that is energetic is also detailed. Whenever an animal respects its habitat, it becomes alert. Being neutral guarantees that the animal is experienced. For the eagle, either it is not detailed or it respects its habitat. For the lynx, either it is not experienced or it is resourceful.
examples.question: Is the park lynx resourceful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the park lynx possesses the property of being resourceful. To answer this, we will use the facts about the lynx's neutrality and the logical connections that link neutrality to experience and experience to resourcefulness.

The rule that neutrality leads to experience is crucial because it lets us translate the known fact about the lynx into a statement about its experience. This connection will be needed later to activate the disjunction involving experience.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the lynx is neutral, then it is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(park_lynx) :- neutral(park_lynx)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "either not experienced or resourceful" sets up a logical choice: if we can show the lynx is experienced, the only remaining way for the statement to be true is that the lynx must be resourceful. Hence this premise directly ties the goal to the lynx's experience.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either the lynx is not experienced or it is resourceful.</text>
<logic>~experienced(park_lynx) v resourceful(park_lynx)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that the lynx is neutral gives us a concrete starting point. Since neutrality is a property we already know, we can apply the rule from Premise 1 to infer something new about the lynx.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The lynx is neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(park_lynx)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, we combine the fact that the lynx is neutral with the rule that neutrality implies experience. This yields the conclusion that the lynx is experienced. This step bridges the gap between a known fact and the condition needed for the disjunction.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Since the lynx is neutral and neutrality implies experience, the lynx is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(park_lynx)</logic>
</step>

Now we have the lynx's experience, which satisfies the left side of the disjunction "not experienced or resourceful". Because the left side is false, the disjunction can only be true if the right side holds, so we infer that the lynx is resourceful. This uses disjunctive syllogism to extract the desired property.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Given that the lynx is experienced, the only way for the disjunction "not experienced or resourceful" to hold is that the lynx is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(park_lynx)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Park_lynx is resourceful, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Hazel is currently feeling relaxed. Whenever something is relaxed, it is also meticulous, so hazel is meticulous. Any meticulous individual is active, which means hazel is active. If someone is active, they are courageous, so hazel is courageous. Being attentive leads to vigor; therefore, anyone who is attentive becomes vigorous. Whenever something is vigorous, it is cautious, so hazel would be cautious if she were vigorous. Cautiousness guarantees brilliance, meaning any cautious being is brilliant. If something is reliable, then it cannot be courageous; in other words, reliability implies not courageous. Finally, hazel satisfies at least one of two possibilities: she is either reliable or attentive.
examples.question: Is hazel brilliant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We start with the information that hazel is relaxed and a collection of conditional statements linking various qualities. The goal is to determine whether hazel can be concluded to be brilliant based on these premises.

This rule tells us that meticulousness is sufficient for activity; if we ever establish hazel's meticulousness, we can immediately infer she is active. It will be used after we derive meticulousness from the relaxed fact.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If hazel is meticulous then hazel is active.</text>
<logic>active(hazel) :- meticulous(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the direct bridge from cautiousness to brilliance. Once we manage to prove hazel is cautious, this premise will allow us to jump straight to the desired brilliant property.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If hazel is cautious then hazel is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(hazel) :- cautious(hazel)</logic>
</step>

This implication connects vigor to cautiousness. It will become useful after we obtain vigor, which itself depends on attentiveness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If hazel is vigorous then hazel is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(hazel) :- vigorous(hazel)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that activity guarantees courage. It will help us later when we need to reason about the incompatibility of courage with reliability.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If hazel is active then hazel is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(hazel) :- active(hazel)</logic>
</step>

This conditional links relaxedness to meticulousness, giving us the first step from the known fact that hazel is relaxed toward the chain of activity and courage.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If hazel is relaxed then hazel is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(hazel) :- relaxed(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Attentiveness leads to vigor, so once we know hazel is attentive we can derive vigor, and then cautiousness via the previous rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If hazel is attentive then hazel is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(hazel) :- attentive(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Reliability forces a negation of courage. This will be crucial for a proof by contradiction that eliminates the possibility of hazel being reliable.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If hazel is reliable then hazel is not courageous.</text>
<logic>~courageous(hazel) :- reliable(hazel)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction asserts that hazel must satisfy at least one of two properties: reliable or attentive. This will allow us to choose attentiveness once we rule out reliability.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Hazel is either reliable or attentive.</text>
<logic>reliable(hazel) v attentive(hazel)</logic>
</step>

The fact that hazel is relaxed is the starting point of the whole derivation. It activates the rule that turns relaxedness into meticulousness.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Hazel is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that hazel is relaxed together with the rule that relaxed implies meticulous, we conclude hazel is meticulous. This gives us the first positive property beyond the initial fact.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>From hazel being relaxed we infer that hazel is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have hazel's meticulousness, we apply the rule that meticulousness implies activity. This step moves us forward along the chain toward courage.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Because hazel is meticulous, we can conclude that hazel is active.</text>
<logic>active(hazel)</logic>
</step>

With hazel established as active, the rule linking activity to courage lets us infer that hazel is courageous. This new fact will later clash with the reliability implication.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>Since hazel is active, we derive that hazel is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(hazel)</logic>
</step>

We know that if hazel were reliable she would not be courageous, but we have just shown she is courageous. Therefore, reliability would produce a contradiction, so we must conclude that hazel cannot be reliable.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Given that hazel is courageous and that reliability would make her not courageous, we infer that hazel cannot be reliable.</text>
<logic>~reliable(hazel)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us hazel is either reliable or attentive. Since we have just ruled out reliability, the only remaining option is attentiveness, so we deduce hazel is attentive.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>Because hazel is not reliable and she must be either reliable or attentive, we deduce that hazel is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Attentiveness triggers vigor according to the relevant rule. Hence, from hazel being attentive we derive that she is vigorous.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,6">
<text>Attentiveness leads to vigor, so hazel is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Vigorousness leads to cautiousness via the rule that vigorous implies cautious. Thus, we obtain hazel's cautiousness, which is the key to reaching brilliance.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,3">
<text>Vigorousness makes hazel cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule that cautiousness guarantees brilliance allows us to conclude that hazel is brilliant. This directly satisfies the query we set out to prove.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,2">
<text>Cautiousness yields brilliance, therefore hazel is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hazel is brilliant, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the study of the ancient site, the researcher recorded that Rio is attentive, Rio is industrious, and Rio is qualified. examples.question: Is Rio graded?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Rio can be classified as graded based on the given facts and default rules. The goal is to see if the conditions for the graded default are satisfied after resolving any conflicts.

This premise introduces a default that turns attentiveness into content, provided there is no specific abnormality blocking it. It will be useful if we can show that the abnormality ab_content_a does not apply to Rio.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The default rule says that if Rio is attentive and no abnormality ab_content_a holds, then Rio is content.</text>
<logic>content(rio) :- attentive(rio), not ab_content_a(rio)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the rule that makes someone graded once they are known to be content, again assuming no abnormality ab_graded. This connects directly to the query, so we must first obtain content(Rio).
<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>graded(rio) :- content(rio), not ab_graded(rio)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The default rule says that if Rio is qualified and no abnormality ab_content_b holds, then Rio is not content.</text>

</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is a known fact that Rio is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(rio)</logic>
</step>

We have two competing defaults: the attentive‑to‑content one and the qualified‑to‑not‑content one. Since the attentive default is given priority, and we know Rio is attentive, we can safely adopt content(Rio) despite the qualified default. Thus we derive that Rio is content.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1,3">
<text>Because Rio is attentive and the attentive‑to‑content default has higher priority than the qualified‑to‑not‑content default, we conclude that Rio is content.</text>
<logic>content(rio)</logic>
</step>

With content(Rio) now established and no ab_graded abnormality mentioned, the graded default fires, allowing us to infer graded(Rio). This step directly uses the result of STEP_5 together with PREMISE_2.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Having established that Rio is content and knowing the graded‑if‑content default, we infer that Rio is graded.</text>
<logic>graded(rio)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Rio is graded, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

We are asked to determine whether Freya can be shown to be not productive. The goal is to use the facts about Freya's state and the conditional rules that connect relaxation, observance, generosity, and productivity to reach a conclusion about her productivity.

<logic>generous(freya) :- observant(freya)</logic>
</step>

The rule that relaxation leads to observance is crucial because we already know Freya is relaxed. By applying this rule we can move from the known fact to the needed observant status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Freya is relaxed then Freya is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(freya) :- relaxed(freya)</logic>
</step>

The productivity‑to‑non‑generosity rule provides the contrapositive bridge we need: if Freya were productive, she would have to be not generous. Once we have generosity, this rule will let us reject the possibility of productivity.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Freya is productive then Freya is not generous.</text>
<logic>~generous(freya) :- productive(freya)</logic>

The explicit fact that Freya is relaxed gives us a concrete starting point. It is the only fact about Freya in the knowledge base, and it will trigger the relaxation‑observance rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">

</step>

<logic>observant(freya)</logic>

Now that we have established Freya as observant, the earlier rule that observance implies generosity lets us conclude that Freya is generous. This new fact sets up the conflict with the productivity rule.

</step>

We have Freya as generous, but the rule says a productive Freya would be not generous. Since generosity and not‑generosity cannot both hold, the only consistent conclusion is that Freya is not productive. This uses a form of modus tollens to negate productivity.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

The disjunction gives us two possibilities: either Blake is not market-focused, or Blake is trendsetting. When we later establish that Blake is market-focused, the only way for the disjunction to stay true is for Blake to be trendsetting.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The statement says that either the designer is not market-focused or the designer is trendsetting.</text>
<logic>~market_focused(designer_blake) v trendsetting(designer_blake)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Blake is skilled is the foundation of the chain; it triggers the rule that skilled implies eclectic, kicking off the forward reasoning process.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is given that designer Blake is skilled.</text>
<logic>skilled(designer_blake)</logic>
</step>

Because Blake is skilled and the rule says skilled leads to eclectic, we can conclude Blake is eclectic. This moves us from a concrete fact to a new property that will be useful for the next implication.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Blake being skilled and the rule that skilled implies eclectic, we infer that Blake is eclectic.</text>
<logic>eclectic(designer_blake)</logic>
</step>

Having Blake as eclectic and using the rule that eclectic implies market-focused, we deduce Blake is market-focused. This gives us the positive side of the disjunction needed for the next step.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Blake being eclectic and the rule that eclectic implies market-focused, we infer that Blake is market-focused.</text>
<logic>market_focused(designer_blake)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that either Blake is not market-focused or Blake is trendsetting. Since we now know Blake is market-focused, the first alternative is false, forcing the second alternative to be true; thus Blake must be trendsetting.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Using the market-focused fact and the disjunction that either not market-focused or trendsetting holds, we conclude that Blake is trendsetting.</text>
<logic>trendsetting(designer_blake)</logic>
</step>

With Blake established as trendsetting, the rule linking trendsetting to masterful allows us to infer that Blake is masterful. This provides the positive counterpart to the negative rule about artisticness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From Blake being trendsetting and the rule that trendsetting implies masterful, we infer that Blake is masterful.</text>
<logic>masterful(designer_blake)</logic>
</step>

We have Blake as masterful, but the rule says that if Blake were artistic, he would not be masterful. Since Blake is masterful, the antecedent of that rule must be false, leading us to conclude Blake is not artistic.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Since Blake is masterful and the rule that artistic implies not masterful, we deduce that Blake cannot be artistic.</text>
<logic>~artistic(designer_blake)</logic>
</step>

We derived Designer_blake is not artistic, so the conclusion Designer_blake is artistic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

Being meticulous guarantees that one is observant, so any meticulous person, such as Victor if he were meticulous, would be observant. Any judicious person is studious, which means Dean's judiciousness makes him studious. If a person is reflective, then they are not ambitious; therefore, a reflective Gail would not be ambitious. examples.question: Is Gail efficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether Gail possesses the property of being efficient. The available facts, rules, and disjunctions about ambition, reflectiveness, and efficiency will allow us to reason toward a definitive answer.

The rule linking reflectiveness to a lack of ambition is crucial because it connects two properties that appear in the other premises. Understanding this conditional helps us later to eliminate the possibility that Gail is reflective.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Gail is reflective, then she is not ambitious.</text>
<logic>~ambitious(gail) :- reflective(gail)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction stating that Gail is either reflective or efficient provides a direct alternative we can evaluate once we know whether she can be reflective. This will be the key step for applying disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either Gail is reflective or Gail is efficient.</text>
<logic>reflective(gail) v efficient(gail)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Gail is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(gail)</logic>
</step>

We know from the facts that Gail is ambitious. The rule tells us that if she were reflective, she would have to be not ambitious. Since she is actually ambitious, the antecedent (being reflective) cannot hold; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Therefore, we can safely infer that Gail is not reflective, using modus tollens.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Since Gail is ambitious and being reflective would make her not ambitious, we can conclude that Gail is not reflective.</text>
<logic>~reflective(gail)</logic>
</step>

Now we have two pieces: the disjunction that Gail is either reflective or efficient, and the derived fact that she is not reflective. By eliminating the reflective option, the only remaining possibility in the disjunction is that Gail is efficient. This follows the pattern of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Given that Gail is not reflective and that either she is reflective or efficient, we infer that Gail must be efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(gail)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Gail is efficient, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is it the case that the sage is not sharp?
examples.completion:

We are asked to determine whether the sage lacks the property of being sharp. To answer, we must use the supplied facts, conditional rules, and the disjunctive statement to see what can be derived about the sage's sharpness.

The second rule links respectful behavior to sharpness, indicating that respect guarantees sharpness. Once we know the sage is respectful, we will be able to infer sharpness directly.

<logic>sharp(sage) :- respectful(sage)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides a choice: either the sage is not principled or the sage is respectful. This logical alternative will become useful after we establish the sage's principled status, allowing us to eliminate the first option and accept the second.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The knowledge base includes the disjunction that the sage is either not principled or respectful.</text>
<logic>~principled(sage) v respectful(sage)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the sage is insightful gives us a concrete starting point. It is the only explicit property supplied, and it will trigger the first conditional rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>insightful(sage)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first rule to the fact that the sage is insightful yields that the sage is principled. This is a straightforward instance of modus ponens: the antecedent holds, so we can assert the consequent.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the fact that the sage is insightful and the rule that insightful implies principled, we infer that the sage is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(sage)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have principled(sage), the disjunction ~principled(sage) v respectful(sage) cannot be satisfied by the first literal, because principled(sage) makes ~principled(sage) false. Therefore, the only way the disjunction remains true is if respectful(sage) holds, giving us respectful(sage) by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Using the principled status of the sage together with the disjunction that either not principled or respectful holds, we deduce that the sage must be respectful.</text>
<logic>respectful(sage)</logic>
</step>

With respectful(sage) established, the second rule applies: any respectful entity is sharp. Using modus ponens again, we conclude sharp(sage).
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that the sage is respectful and the rule that respectful implies sharp, we conclude that the sage is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(sage)</logic>
</step>

We derived Sage is sharp, so the conclusion Sage is not sharp is False.
</think>

examples.question: Is Grant studious?
examples.completion:

The third premise provides a contrapositive relationship: cleverness would force Grant to be not goal oriented. Since we have a fact about his goal orientation, this rule will let us reason about his cleverness.

</step>

Because the rule says cleverness leads to ¬goal_oriented and we know Grant is goal_oriented, the only way to avoid contradiction is to reject the antecedent. Hence, Grant must not be clever. This step uses a classic modus tollens pattern.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">

<logic>~clever(grant)</logic>

Having established ¬clever(Grant), we can now apply the rule from PREMISE_1: ¬clever(Grant) → perceptive(Grant). Therefore, Grant is perceptive. This follows directly by modus ponens.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From the conclusion that Grant is not clever and the rule linking non‑cleverness to perceptiveness, we infer that Grant is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(grant)</logic>

With perceptive(Grant) in hand, we invoke the rule from PREMISE_2, which states that perceptiveness entails studiousness. Consequently, Grant is studious. This again is a straightforward modus ponens application.

examples.question: Is the cataloger sharp?

</step>

Because Taylor is efficient, the clause "Taylor is not efficient" in the disjunction is false. To keep the disjunction true, the other option—Taylor is loyal—must be true. Hence we conclude that Taylor is loyal.

<text>From the fact that Taylor is efficient and the disjunction that either Taylor is not efficient or loyal, we infer that Taylor must be loyal.</text>

Now that we know Taylor is loyal, the left side of the second disjunction ("Taylor is not loyal") is false. Therefore the right side, "Taylor is inventive," must hold, giving us inventiveness for Taylor.

</step>

We now possess the fact that Taylor is studious. The third rule tells us that capability would make Taylor not studious. Since Taylor is indeed studious, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to assert that Taylor is not capable.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Because Taylor is studious and the rule that capability forces non‑studiousness, we infer that Taylor is not capable.</text>
<logic>~capable(taylor)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Taylor is not capable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The blueprint is systematic planning. Any blueprint that is systematic planning is also structurally sound. Whenever a blueprint has flexible design, it enjoys aesthetic balance. Either the blueprint is not structurally sound, or it has flexible design.
examples.question: Is the blueprint aesthetically balanced?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the blueprint possesses aesthetic balance. To answer this, we must trace how the given facts, rules, and the disjunction interact and lead to the desired property.

The first rule tells us that systematic planning guarantees structural soundness. This connection will let us move from the known fact about the blueprint's planning style to a statement about its structural status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any systematic planning implies structural soundness.</text>
<logic>structurally_sound(blueprint) :- systematic_planning(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links flexible design to aesthetic balance. It will be essential later when we obtain flexible design for the blueprint, allowing us to infer aesthetic balance.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any flexible design implies aesthetic balance.</text>
<logic>aesthetic_balance(blueprint) :- flexible_design(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either the blueprint lacks structural soundness, or it exhibits flexible design. Once we know the blueprint is structurally sound, we can eliminate the first option and deduce the second.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the blueprint is not structurally sound, or it has flexible design.</text>
<logic>~structurally_sound(blueprint) v flexible_design(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that the blueprint is systematic planning. This fact serves as the starting point for applying the first rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The blueprint is systematic planning.</text>
<logic>systematic_planning(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the blueprint is systematic planning together with the rule that systematic planning implies structural soundness, we can conclude the blueprint is structurally sound. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the blueprint's systematic planning and the rule linking systematic planning to structural soundness, we infer that the blueprint is structurally sound.</text>
<logic>structurally_sound(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the blueprint is structurally sound, the negated part of the disjunction (~structurally sound) is false. Therefore, to satisfy the disjunction, the remaining alternative must be true, giving us that the blueprint has flexible design. This follows the principle of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Since the blueprint is structurally sound, the first part of the disjunction (~structurally sound) is false, so the alternative must hold: the blueprint has flexible design.</text>
<logic>flexible_design(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Having derived that the blueprint has flexible design, we can invoke the second rule which says flexible design leads to aesthetic balance. By applying modus ponens again, we infer that the blueprint is aesthetically balanced.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given the blueprint's flexible design and the rule that flexible design leads to aesthetic balance, we conclude the blueprint is aesthetically balanced.</text>
<logic>aesthetic_balance(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Blueprint is aesthetic balance, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Hazel, a celebrated fashion designer, finds great satisfaction in her latest runway collection; her work is described as content. In the fashion world, any collection that is content is also considered resilient, meaning it can withstand changing trends. Regarding her personal style, it is known that either Hazel is not empathetic, or she maintains a consistent approach to her designs. Moreover, either Hazel lacks resilience, or she demonstrates empathy in her collaborations. These statements together capture all the premises we will use.
examples.question: Is Hazel consistent in her design approach?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Hazel's design approach is consistent. The problem supplies a fact about her work, a conditional rule, and two disjunctive statements that together allow us to infer new properties step by step.

The conditional rule connects the notion of being content with being resilient. Recognizing this link is essential because it will let us turn the given fact about content into a statement about resilience later on.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any content collection by Hazel is also resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(hazel) :- content(hazel)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction gives us a choice: either Hazel lacks empathy or she is consistent. This will become useful after we establish whether she is empathetic, because the disjunction will then force the consistent side.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>One of the possibilities is that Hazel is not empathetic, or else she is consistent in her design philosophy.</text>
<logic>~empathetic(hazel) v consistent(hazel)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers another alternative: either Hazel is not resilient or she is empathetic. Once we know Hazel's resilience status, we can use this clause to infer her empathy.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Hazel is not resilient, or she shows empathy.</text>
<logic>~resilient(hazel) v empathetic(hazel)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact tells us that Hazel's collection is content. This is the starting point for our chain of reasoning, as it activates the conditional rule about resilience.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Hazel's collection is content.</text>
<logic>content(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Because we have the fact that Hazel's work is content and the rule that content implies resilience, we can directly infer that her collection is resilient. This application of modus ponens turns the abstract rule into a concrete property of Hazel.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since Hazel's work is content and the rule links content to resilience, we can conclude her collection is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Hazel is resilient, the disjunction "not resilient or empathetic" can no longer be satisfied by the first option. Since the resilient case rules out "not resilient," the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Hazel to be empathetic. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Given that Hazel is resilient and the second disjunction says either she is not resilient or she is empathetic, the resilient case eliminates the first alternative, leaving us with empathy.</text>
<logic>empathetic(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Hazel is empathetic, we return to the earlier disjunction "not empathetic or consistent." The empathetic condition makes the first alternative false, so the disjunction can only be true if Hazel is consistent. Thus we conclude her consistency.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>With Hazel now established as empathetic, the first disjunction (~empathetic or consistent) forces the consistent alternative, so Hazel's approach is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(hazel)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hazel is consistent, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Coco is alert. Whiskers is also alert. Whenever Coco is friendly, Coco is obedient. If Coco is alert, then Coco is fair. If Coco is fair, then Coco is trainable. Either Coco is not trainable or Coco is friendly. These statements capture all the given facts, rules, and the disjunction.
examples.question: Is Coco obedient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several properties of the animal Coco and a set of logical relationships among them. Our goal is to determine whether Coco is obedient based on the information provided.

The first premise tells us that friendliness leads directly to obedience for Coco. This will be useful later when we manage to establish that Coco is friendly.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Coco is friendly then Coco is obedient.</text>
<logic>obedient(coco) :- friendly(coco)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links alertness to fairness, meaning any alert animal like Coco will also be fair. This creates a bridge from a known fact to another property we need.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Coco is alert then Coco is fair.</text>
<logic>fair(coco) :- alert(coco)</logic>
</step>

The third premise states that fairness guarantees trainability. Once we know Coco is fair, we can immediately obtain trainability, moving us forward in the chain.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Coco is fair then Coco is trainable.</text>
<logic>trainable(coco) :- fair(coco)</logic>
</step>

The disjunctive premise offers two alternatives: either Coco is not trainable or Coco is friendly. This logical ‘or’ will allow us to infer friendliness once we can rule out the first option.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Coco is not trainable or Coco is friendly.</text>
<logic>~trainable(coco) v friendly(coco)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Coco is alert. This fact is the starting point for applying the conditional rules that follow.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Coco is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(coco)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Coco is alert together with the rule that alertness implies fairness, we can conclude Coco is fair. This step converts the known property into a new one needed for the next implication.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Coco being alert and the rule that alert implies fair, we infer that Coco is fair.</text>
<logic>fair(coco)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Coco is fair, we apply the rule that fairness leads to trainability, thereby deducing that Coco is trainable. This fulfills the antecedent of the disjunction in PREMISE_4.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since Coco is fair and fair implies trainable, we conclude Coco is trainable.</text>
<logic>trainable(coco)</logic>
</step>

Now we know Coco is trainable, which makes the first part of the disjunction (~trainable) false. Consequently, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second part, Coco being friendly, to be true. Thus we infer Coco is friendly.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given that Coco is trainable and the disjunction that Coco is not trainable or friendly, we deduce Coco must be friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(coco)</logic>
</step>

With Coco confirmed as friendly, we can finally use the rule that friendliness yields obedience. This gives us the desired conclusion that Coco is obedient.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because Coco is friendly and friendly implies obedient, we derive that Coco is obedient.</text>
<logic>obedient(coco)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Coco is obedient, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True
