Answer: False

examples.context: In the mythic realm, Hermes is described as fated and wise. There is no known abnormality that would block his qualification or his rejuvenation. Whenever Hermes is rejuvenated, he becomes enduring. If Hermes is enduring and no abnormality prevents qualification, then he is a chosen_one. If Hermes is wise and no abnormality prevents rejuvenation, then he is rejuvenated. Hermes is wise.
examples.question: Is it the case that Hermes is not a chosen_one?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Hermes can be said not to be a chosen_one. To answer this, we will follow the chain of facts and default rules provided in the mythic description, deriving intermediate properties until we reach a conclusion about his qualification status.

The rule linking rejuvenation to endurance tells us that any time Hermes is found to be rejuvenated, we may immediately consider him enduring. This connection will be used later once we establish that he is rejuvenated.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Hermes is rejuvenated, then he is enduring.</text>
<logic>enduring(hermes) :- rejuvenated(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The qualification rule states that endurance, together with the absence of any abnormality, guarantees that Hermes is a chosen_one. It sets up the final step where, if we can show endurance, we will be able to claim qualification.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Hermes is enduring and there is no abnormality preventing qualification, he is a chosen_one.</text>
<logic>chosen_one(hermes) :- enduring(hermes), not ab_chosen_one(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The rejuvenation rule provides a way to obtain the rejuvenated property from wisdom, again assuming no abnormality interferes. Since we already know Hermes is wise, this rule is the bridge to get him rejuvenated.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Hermes is wise and there is no abnormality preventing rejuvenation, then he is rejuvenated.</text>
<logic>rejuvenated(hermes) :- wise(hermes), not ab_rejuvenated(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Hermes is wise is given directly. This piece of information is essential because it activates the rejuvenation default rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Hermes is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(hermes)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that Hermes is wise with the rejuvenation rule, and noting that no abnormality prevents rejuvenation, we can conclude that Hermes is rejuvenated. This follows the default reasoning pattern where a condition and the lack of an exception yield the consequent.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Since Hermes is wise and no abnormality blocks rejuvenation, we infer that he is rejuvenated.</text>
<logic>rejuvenated(hermes)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Hermes is rejuvenated, the earlier rule about rejuvenation implying endurance allows us to infer that Hermes is enduring. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens: the antecedent of the rule holds, so the consequent must hold as well.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Because Hermes is rejuvenated, the earlier rule gives that he is enduring.</text>
<logic>enduring(hermes)</logic>
</step>

With Hermes now known to be enduring, and remembering that there is no abnormality blocking qualification, the qualification rule lets us derive that Hermes is a chosen_one. The default rule activates because both the positive condition (endurance) and the negative condition (no abnormality) are satisfied.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that Hermes is enduring and no abnormality stops qualification, we conclude that he is a chosen_one.</text>
<logic>chosen_one(hermes)</logic>
</step>

We derived Hermes is chosen one, so the conclusion Hermes is not chosen one is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the pottery studio, David, Jane, and Lara are all impartial, and each of them is punctual as well. Nadia is also punctual. The workshop has a rule that if Nadia is impartial, then Nadia is astute. Another rule says that whenever Nadia is punctual, she is focused. A further rule states that if Nadia is astute, then Nadia is skillful. There is also a rule that if Nadia is determined, then Nadia is not focused. Finally, the information given includes the disjunction that either Nadia is determined or Nadia is impartial (or possibly both).
examples.question: Based on the given information, is Nadia skillful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Nadia possesses the skillful quality. To answer, we must chain together the facts and conditional rules provided about Nadia's punctuality, focus, determination, impartiality, and astuteness.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Nadia is impartial, then Nadia is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(nadia) :- impartial(nadia)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Nadia is punctual, Nadia is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(nadia) :- punctual(nadia)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Nadia is astute, then Nadia is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(nadia) :- astute(nadia)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Nadia is determined, then Nadia is not focused.</text>
<logic>~focused(nadia) :- determined(nadia)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Nadia is determined or Nadia is impartial.</text>
<logic>determined(nadia) v impartial(nadia)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Nadia is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(nadia)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Nadia is punctual directly activates the rule linking punctuality to focus. Because the rule states that punctual implies focused, we can safely assert that Nadia is focused. This establishes the first needed property for later steps.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Nadia being punctual we infer that Nadia is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(nadia)</logic>
</step>

We also have a rule that if Nadia were determined, she would not be focused. Since we have just shown Nadia is focused, the contrapositive reasoning (modus tollens) tells us that Nadia cannot be determined. Hence we conclude that Nadia is not determined.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Because Nadia is focused and the rule that determined implies not focused, we conclude that Nadia is not determined.</text>
<logic>~determined(nadia)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that at least one of "Nadia is determined" or "Nadia is impartial" must be true. Having established that Nadia is not determined, the only remaining option is that Nadia is impartial. This uses disjunctive syllogism to eliminate the false disjunct.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Given that Nadia is not determined and that either determined or impartial holds, we deduce that Nadia is impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(nadia)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Nadia is impartial, we can apply the rule that impartiality leads to astuteness for Nadia. The rule allows us to directly infer that Nadia is astute from her impartial status.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Since Nadia is impartial, we can infer that Nadia is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(nadia)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule stating that astuteness guarantees skillfulness lets us conclude that Nadia is skillful, because we have just derived that she is astute.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>With Nadia being astute, we derive that Nadia is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(nadia)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Nadia is skillful, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Oak_beam is lightweight, and so is saw_john, hammer_olga, and planer_sage. Nail_gwen always arrives on_time, while level_troy is trusted by everyone. Whenever someone is lightweight, they are also sharp. Whenever something is sharp, it is careful. If someone is precise, then they are not careful. If a carpenter arrives on_time, they are steady. If a carpenter is orderly, they become driven. Anyone who is trusted is straight, and anyone who is sturdy becomes preemptive. Additionally, either nail_gwen is not steady or nail_gwen is orderly, and either level_troy is not straight or level_troy is sturdy.
examples.question: Is it true that saw_john is not precise?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a set of properties about several carpenters and a handful of logical relationships among those properties. Our goal is to determine whether the carpenter named Saw John lacks the property of being precise.

The rule that any lightweight carpenter is sharp links the concept of being light in weight to possessing a sharp edge. This connection will allow us to move from a fact about weight to a fact about sharpness later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any carpenter who is lightweight is also sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(saw_john) :- lightweight(saw_john)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that sharpness guarantees carefulness. By establishing sharpness for Saw John, we will be able to derive his carefulness, which is crucial for the next inference.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Any sharp carpenter is careful.</text>
<logic>careful(saw_john) :- sharp(saw_john)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a negation: if a carpenter were precise, they could not be careful. This relationship will let us turn a statement about carefulness into a statement about the absence of precision.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a carpenter is precise, then they are not careful.</text>
<logic>~careful(saw_john) :- precise(saw_john)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Saw John is lightweight. This concrete fact serves as the starting point for applying the first rule about lightweight implying sharpness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Saw John is lightweight.</text>
<logic>lightweight(saw_john)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Saw John is lightweight together with the rule that lightweight implies sharp, we can directly conclude that Saw John is sharp. This step bridges the initial fact to a property needed for the next rule.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From Saw John's lightweight nature we infer that he is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(saw_john)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Saw John is sharp, we now apply the rule that sharpness leads to carefulness. Consequently, we can assert that Saw John is careful, giving us a positive attribute that will be used in a negation argument.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Because Saw John is sharp, we can conclude that he is careful.</text>
<logic>careful(saw_john)</logic>
</step>

We now have Saw John being careful, and we also have the rule that any precise carpenter would not be careful. By contraposing this rule, the presence of carefulness forces the absence of precision, allowing us to infer that Saw John is not precise.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since Saw John is careful and a precise carpenter would not be careful, we deduce that Saw John cannot be precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(saw_john)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Saw_john is not precise, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The silk_scarf is data_informed. Either the silk_scarf is not data_informed, or it is inspired. If the silk_scarf is inspired, then it is timeless. If the silk_scarf is trend_friendly, then it is not timeless.
examples.question: Is the silk_scarf trend_friendly?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the silk_scarf possesses the trend_friendly quality. The goal is to see if the available facts and rules force the scarf to be trend_friendly or not.

The rule linking inspiration to timelessness is important because later we will establish that the scarf is inspired, allowing us to infer timelessness. This connection will later clash with the rule about trend_friendly scarves.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the silk_scarf is inspired, then it is timeless.</text>
<logic>timeless(silk_scarf) :- inspired(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that being trend_friendly would make the scarf not timeless. Since we will later prove the scarf is timeless, this rule will help us rule out the trend_friendly possibility.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the silk_scarf is trend_friendly, then it is not timeless.</text>
<logic>~timeless(silk_scarf) :- trend_friendly(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either the scarf is not data_informed or it is inspired. Because we already know the scarf is data_informed, this statement will let us eliminate the first alternative and deduce inspiration.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the silk_scarf is not data_informed, or it is inspired.</text>
<logic>~data_informed(silk_scarf) v inspired(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The silk_scarf is data_informed.</text>
<logic>data_informed(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that the silk_scarf is data_informed, we can reject the "not data_informed" part of the disjunction. With that option removed, the remaining clause must be true, so the scarf is inspired. This uses disjunctive syllogism to turn the disjunction into a concrete fact.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because the silk_scarf is data_informed, the alternative that it is not data_informed must be false, so the disjunction forces the silk_scarf to be inspired.</text>
<logic>inspired(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the scarf is inspired, we can apply the earlier rule that any inspired scarf is timeless. By modus ponens, the premise "if inspired then timeless" gives us the conclusion that the silk_scarf is timeless.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since the silk_scarf is inspired, the rule that an inspired scarf is timeless lets us conclude that the silk_scarf is timeless.</text>
<logic>timeless(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

We have the scarf being timeless, but the rule for trend_friendly scarves says a trend_friendly scarf would be not timeless. Using modus tollens, the truth of timelessness forces the negation of the antecedent, meaning the scarf cannot be trend_friendly.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that the silk_scarf is timeless, the rule that a trend_friendly scarf would be not timeless cannot hold, so the silk_scarf cannot be trend_friendly.</text>
<logic>~trend_friendly(silk_scarf)</logic>
</step>

We derived Silk_scarf is not trend friendly, so the conclusion Silk_scarf is trend friendly is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Seth is known to be thorough. Whenever someone is thorough, they are systematic. Whenever someone is systematic, they are articulate. If a person is articulate, then they are content. If a person is healthy, then they are empathetic. If a person is impartial, then they are not content. Moreover, Seth is either impartial or healthy, but we do not know which. These statements capture all the facts, conditionals, and the single disjunction that will be used in the proof.
examples.question: Is it true that Seth is not empathetic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Seth lacks empathy. The goal is to see if the proposition ~empathetic(seth) can be supported from the given information. To do this, we will follow the chain of rules and facts step by step, ultimately checking whether empathy can be derived for Seth.

The first rule links systematic behavior to articulate speech. Recognizing this connection is important because later we will establish Seth as systematic, which will let us infer his articulateness. This premise gives us a conditional bridge we will later cross.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Seth is systematic, then Seth is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(seth) :- systematic(seth)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects being articulate with being content. Once we know Seth is articulate, this rule will allow us to claim he is content. It sets up a second conditional that will be useful after we derive articulateness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Seth is articulate, then Seth is content.</text>
<logic>content(seth) :- articulate(seth)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that health guarantees empathy. If we ever manage to prove Seth is healthy, this rule will let us directly infer his empathy. It is the final link we need to reach the target predicate.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Seth is healthy, then Seth is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(seth) :- healthy(seth)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule states that thoroughness implies systematic work. Since we already have a fact that Seth is thorough, this rule will let us start the chain of derivations by giving us systematicness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Seth is thorough, then Seth is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(seth) :- thorough(seth)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule says that being impartial forces a lack of contentment. This will later help us contradict the possibility that Seth is impartial, once we discover he is content. It is a crucial piece for applying modus tollens.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Seth is impartial, then Seth is not content.</text>
<logic>~content(seth) :- impartial(seth)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise offers a simple disjunction: Seth is either impartial or healthy. This binary choice will become decisive after we eliminate one of the options, allowing us to infer the other.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The premise states that either Seth is impartial or Seth is healthy.</text>
<logic>impartial(seth) v healthy(seth)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise is an explicit fact that Seth is thorough. This concrete piece of information is the starting point for applying the rule that thoroughness leads to systematic behavior.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that Seth is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(seth)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Seth is thorough together with the rule that thoroughness implies systematic, we can deduce that Seth is systematic. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens, turning a general conditional into a specific instance for Seth.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>From the fact that Seth is thorough and the rule that thorough implies systematic, we infer that Seth is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(seth)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Seth is systematic, we now invoke the rule that systematic individuals are articulate. By modus ponens again, we obtain that Seth is articulate, moving us further along the chain toward contentment.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Using Seth's systematic status and the rule that systematic implies articulate, we deduce that Seth is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(seth)</logic>
</step>

Now that Seth is known to be articulate, the rule connecting articulateness to content lets us infer that Seth is content. This step closes the first half of the chain, giving us a concrete predicate that will clash with the impartial rule later.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Given that Seth is articulate and the rule that articulate implies content, we conclude that Seth is content.</text>
<logic>content(seth)</logic>
</step>

We see a tension: the rule says if Seth were impartial, he would not be content, yet we have just shown he is content. Applying modus tollens, we conclude that Seth cannot be impartial. This elimination is pivotal because it clears the way to use the disjunction.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since Seth is content and the rule that impartial leads to not content, we apply modus tollens to obtain that Seth is not impartial.</text>
<logic>~impartial(seth)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Seth is not impartial and the original disjunction that he is either impartial or healthy, we can apply disjunctive syllogism. The only remaining viable option is that Seth is healthy, so we derive his healthiness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>From the fact that Seth is not impartial together with the disjunction that he is either impartial or healthy, we infer by disjunctive syllogism that Seth must be healthy.</text>
<logic>healthy(seth)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the rule that health leads to empathy, we combine Seth's newly proven health with this conditional to obtain that Seth is empathetic. This completes the forward chain from the initial facts to the target predicate.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>With Seth being healthy and the rule that healthy implies empathetic, we derive that Seth is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(seth)</logic>
</step>

We derived Seth is empathetic, so the conclusion Seth is not empathetic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Avery is cheerful. If Avery is content, then Avery is cautious. If Avery is resilient, then Avery is not cautious. Either Avery is not cheerful or Avery is responsible. Either Avery is not responsible or Avery is content.
examples.question: Is Avery resilient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Avery possesses the property of resilience. The available information includes a fact about Avery's cheerfulness, two conditional rules, and two exclusive alternatives, all of which will be used to reason about resilience.

The first premise links content to cautiousness: any time Avery is described as content, it guarantees that Avery is also cautious. This will let us turn a derived content statement into a cautious one later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that whenever Avery is content, Avery must also be cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(avery) :- content(avery)</logic>
</step>

The second premise provides the contrapositive relationship between resilience and cautiousness: if Avery were resilient, then Avery would necessarily be not cautious. This rule will be essential for applying modus tollens once we have a statement about Avery's cautiousness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Avery is resilient, then Avery cannot be cautious.</text>
<logic>~cautious(avery) :- resilient(avery)</logic>
</step>

The third premise is a disjunction stating that at least one of the two conditions holds: either Avery is not cheerful or Avery is responsible. Because we already know Avery is cheerful, the first alternative is false, forcing the second to be true.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>One of the following must hold: Avery is not cheerful, or Avery is responsible.</text>
<logic>~cheerful(avery) v responsible(avery)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise offers another disjunction: either Avery is not responsible or Avery is content. Once we establish Avery's responsibility, the first option becomes impossible, so we can infer Avery's contentness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Avery is not responsible, or Avery is content.</text>
<logic>~responsible(avery) v content(avery)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Avery is cheerful is given directly. This concrete fact will be used to eliminate the negated cheerful side of the first disjunction.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Avery is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(avery)</logic>
</step>

Since we know Avery is cheerful, the clause "Avery is not cheerful" in the first disjunction cannot hold. By disjunctive syllogism, the remaining possibility must be true, so we conclude that Avery is responsible.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From Avery being cheerful and the first disjunction, we infer that Avery is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(avery)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained Avery's responsibility, we look at the second disjunction. The alternative "Avery is not responsible" is now false, leaving "Avery is content" as the only viable option. Thus, we infer that Avery is content.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Using Avery's responsibility together with the second disjunction, we deduce that Avery is content.</text>
<logic>content(avery)</logic>
</step>

With Avery established as content, we can apply the conditional from PREMISE_1. Modus ponens tells us that content implies cautiousness, so we deduce that Avery is cautious.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Because Avery is content, the conditional rule gives us that Avery is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(avery)</logic>
</step>

We now have Avery cautious, and PREMISE_2 tells us that resilience would imply not cautious. Using modus tollens, the presence of cautiousness forces the negation of the antecedent, leading us to conclude that Avery cannot be resilient.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since we now have Avery cautious and the rule linking resilience to non‑cautiousness, we conclude that Avery cannot be resilient.</text>
<logic>~resilient(avery)</logic>
</step>

We derived Avery is not resilient, so the conclusion Avery is resilient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Granite is high_pressure. Shale is fine_grained. Basalt is durable. Whenever granite is high_pressure, it is igneous. If shale is fine_grained, then shale is layered. If shale is weathered, then shale is bright. If granite is stable, then granite is resource_rich. If basalt is porous, then basalt is crystalline. If basalt is crystalline, then basalt is not durable. Granite is either not igneous or it is stable. Shale is either not layered or it is weathered.
examples.question: Is basalt not porous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether basalt lacks porosity. The goal is to show that the predicate porous does not hold for basalt based on the given geological facts and conditional relationships.

The first rule tells us that porosity leads to a crystalline structure in basalt. This connection will be useful because later we will have information about the crystalline property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If basalt is porous, then basalt is crystalline.</text>
<logic>crystalline(basalt) :- porous(basalt)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that a crystalline basalt cannot be durable; instead, it would be not durable. This creates a tension with the fact that basalt is known to be durable.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If basalt is crystalline, then basalt is not durable.</text>
<logic>~durable(basalt) :- crystalline(basalt)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that basalt is durable, providing a solid fact to contrast with the implication from the previous rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Basalt is durable.</text>
<logic>durable(basalt)</logic>
</step>

Given that a crystalline basalt would be not durable, but we know basalt is durable, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to deny that basalt is crystalline. Thus, by modus tollens, we derive that basalt is not crystalline.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because basalt is durable and a crystalline basalt would be not durable, we conclude that basalt is not crystalline.</text>
<logic>~crystalline(basalt)</logic>
</step>

Now we have that basalt is not crystalline, and the earlier rule says that if basalt were porous it would necessarily be crystalline. Since the consequent (crystalline) is false, the antecedent (porous) must also be false. Hence we conclude basalt is not porous.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since basalt is not crystalline and being porous would make it crystalline, we infer that basalt is not porous.</text>
<logic>~porous(basalt)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Basalt is not porous, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the study of four speakers—Hollis, Justice, Mae, and Sutton—each of them is known to feel grateful. Specifically, Hollis, Justice, Mae, and Sutton are all grateful. Moreover, Hollis, Justice, and Sutton are described as proactive, while no proactive claim is made about Mae. The linguistic model includes several inference rules concerning Mae: whenever Mae is proactive, she is decisive; whenever Mae is trustworthy, she is friendly; and whenever Mae is grateful, she is trustworthy. Additionally, there is a disjunctive statement about Mae: either Mae is not friendly or Mae is proactive. No other default assumptions are present.
examples.question: Is Mae decisive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Mae can be concluded to be decisive. To answer, we will use the given facts and inference rules concerning gratitude, trustworthiness, friendliness, and proactivity.

The first rule tells us that proactivity directly leads to decisiveness for Mae. This connection will be essential later when we manage to establish that Mae is proactive.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Mae is proactive, she is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(mae) :- proactive(mae)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links trustworthiness to friendliness for Mae. Knowing that Mae is trustworthy will therefore allow us to assert her friendliness, which is needed for the disjunctive reasoning step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Mae is trustworthy, then Mae is friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(mae) :- trustworthy(mae)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that gratitude guarantees trustworthiness for Mae. Since we already have a fact that Mae is grateful, this rule gives us a way to obtain the trustworthiness predicate.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Mae is grateful, then Mae is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(mae) :- grateful(mae)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two possibilities: either Mae is not friendly, or she is proactive. Once we know which of these holds, we can deduce the other component needed for the final conclusion.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Mae is not friendly, or Mae is proactive.</text>
<logic>~friendly(mae) v proactive(mae)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Mae is grateful anchors the chain of reasoning. It is the starting point from which we will derive trustworthiness and then friendliness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Mae is grateful.</text>
<logic>grateful(mae)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that gratefulness implies trustworthiness, we turn the fact of Mae's gratitude into the new fact that Mae is trustworthy. This step is straightforward use of the implication provided.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Because Mae is grateful, we can infer that Mae is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(mae)</logic>
</step>

With Mae now known to be trustworthy, we invoke the second rule which says trustworthy individuals are friendly. Hence we can confidently state that Mae is friendly.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since Mae is trustworthy, she must be friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(mae)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Mae is friendly, which makes the left side of the disjunction false. Because a disjunction is true when at least one side is true, the falsity of "not friendly" forces the right side—Mae is proactive—to be true.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given that Mae is friendly, the disjunction forces her to be proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(mae)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have derived Mae's proactivity, we can finally use the first rule that proactive individuals are decisive. This yields the desired conclusion that Mae is decisive.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>With Mae being proactive, we conclude that Mae is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(mae)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Mae is decisive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Bella is adaptable, and because of that she is supportive. Carl is loyal, which guarantees that he is also eloquent. Alex is scholarly, and Diana is scholarly as well. Any person who is scholarly is also skillful, so Alex’s scholarly nature makes him skillful. Whenever someone is skillful, they become impartial; therefore Alex’s skillfulness would render him impartial. If Alex were confident, that confidence would force him to be not impartial. Being friendly ensures that Bella is patient. If Carl is open_minded, then he becomes proactive. Either Carl is not eloquent or Carl is open_minded. Either Bella is not supportive or Bella is friendly.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude that Alex is not confident?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Alex lacks confidence. The goal is to see if the premises force the negation of the confident predicate for Alex.

The first premise links skillfulness to impartiality, which will be useful once we know Alex is skillful. Recognizing this connection helps us later derive impartiality for Alex.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is skillful, then they are impartial; applied to Alex, skillful implies impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(alex) :- skillful(alex)</logic>
</step>

The second premise converts scholarly status into skillfulness. Since we already have Alex as scholarly, this rule will let us obtain the skillful fact required for the first premise.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Another rule states that any scholarly person is skillful, so Alex being scholarly gives him skillfulness.</text>
<logic>skillful(alex) :- scholarly(alex)</logic>
</step>

The third premise tells us that confidence would contradict impartiality. This contrapositive relationship is the key to turning an impartiality fact into a statement about confidence.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>A conditional asserts that confidence leads to not being impartial; thus if Alex were confident, he would be not impartial.</text>
<logic>~impartial(alex) :- confident(alex)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that Alex is scholarly, providing the factual basis needed to start the chain of deductions.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We are told directly that Alex is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(alex)</logic>
</step>

By combining the fact that Alex is scholarly with the rule that scholarly implies skillful, we can conclude Alex is skillful. This step supplies the antecedent needed for the impartiality rule.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From Alex being scholarly and the rule that scholarly implies skillful, we infer that Alex is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(alex)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Alex as skillful, the first premise gives us that any skillful individual is impartial, so Alex must be impartial. This establishes the condition that will be used against the confidence rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Alex is now known to be skillful and skillful leads to impartial, we conclude Alex is impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(alex)</logic>
</step>

We have Alex as impartial, but the third premise says confidence would make him not impartial. Since Alex is indeed impartial, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to assert that Alex is not confident. Thus we derive the negation of confidence.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given Alex is impartial and that confidence would make him not impartial, we deduce Alex cannot be confident, i.e., Alex is not confident.</text>
<logic>~confident(alex)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Alex is not confident, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: George has been noted as prepared. Whenever someone is prepared, they are also cheerful, so George’s preparation makes him cheerful. Any cheerful individual is automatically observant, which means George is observant as a result of his cheerfulness. If a creature is skillful, then it is perceptive; therefore skillfulness guarantees perceptiveness. Being polite forces a creature to lack perceptiveness, so any polite person is not perceptive. Moreover, either George is not observant or he is skillful – at least one of these two alternatives must hold. No other default assumptions are made.
examples.question: Is George polite?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether George is polite based on the information provided. To answer, we will follow the chain of implications and the given disjunction, step by step, until we can assess the truth of the politeness claim.

The first rule tells us that preparation guarantees cheerfulness. This connection will let us move from the known fact that George is prepared to a new property, which is essential for building the reasoning chain. Recognizing this implication early helps us plan the next inference.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If George is prepared then George is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(george) :- prepared(george)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links cheerfulness to observance. Once we have established George's cheerfulness, this rule will allow us to derive that he is observant, pushing the argument forward toward the crucial disjunction. It sets up a necessary intermediate step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If George is cheerful then George is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(george) :- cheerful(george)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that skillfulness implies perceptiveness. This will become useful after we manage to prove George is skillful, giving us a route to obtain perceptiveness later in the proof. It prepares us for a later conversion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If George is skillful then George is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(george) :- skillful(george)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that if someone is polite, then they cannot be perceptive. This contrapositive relationship will later enable us to infer the negation of politeness once we have perceptiveness for George. It is the key to reaching the final conclusion.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If George is polite then George is not perceptive.</text>
<logic>~perceptive(george) :- polite(george)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two possibilities: either George is not observant, or he is skillful. This logical alternative will be resolved once we know George's observance status, allowing us to deduce skillfulness if the first option is ruled out. It creates a branching point that we will close.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either George is not observant or George is skillful.</text>
<logic>~observant(george) v skillful(george)</logic>
</step>

The fact that George is prepared is our starting point. It directly activates the first rule and initiates the forward chaining process. Without this fact, none of the subsequent derivations would be grounded.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>George is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(george)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first rule to the fact that George is prepared yields George being cheerful. This is a straightforward use of modus ponens: the antecedent holds, so the consequent must hold as well. This new fact will feed into the next rule.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From George being prepared and the rule that preparation implies cheerfulness, we infer that George is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(george)</logic>
</step>

With George now known to be cheerful, the second rule gives us that George is observant. Again, modus ponens allows us to move from the satisfied antecedent (cheerful) to its consequent (observant). This result is crucial for handling the disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Using George’s cheerfulness together with the rule that cheerfulness leads to observance, we deduce that George is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(george)</logic>
</step>

We have established that George is observant, which makes the left side of the disjunction false. Since the disjunction must be true, the only way to satisfy it is for the right side—skillful—to be true. Thus, by disjunctive syllogism we infer that George is skillful.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Given that George is observant and the disjunction that he is either not observant or skillful, we resolve the latter alternative and conclude that George is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(george)</logic>
</step>

Now that George is skillful, the third rule lets us conclude that he is perceptive. This is another direct application of modus ponens, converting skillfulness into perceptiveness. This property will be pivotal for the final negation of politeness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Because George is skillful and skillfulness guarantees perceptiveness, we conclude that George is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(george)</logic>
</step>

We possess the fact that George is perceptive, while the fourth rule tells us that politeness would force a lack of perceptiveness. Using modus tollens, if politeness implied not perceptive, but perceptive actually holds, the antecedent (polite) must be false. Hence we infer that George is not polite.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Since George is perceptive and the rule states that politeness would make someone not perceptive, we infer that George cannot be polite.</text>
<logic>~polite(george)</logic>
</step>

We derived George is not polite, so the conclusion George is polite is False.
</think>
Answer: False

<logic>content(avery)</logic>

Using the fact that Avery is content together with the rule that content implies perceptive, we deduce that Avery must be perceptive. This step transforms a static fact into a dynamic property that can trigger further rules.

With Avery now known to be enthusiastic, the rule linking enthusiasm to humility allows us to conclude that Avery is humble. This new humility will be pivotal for obtaining precision.

examples.context: In this design studio, Val is determined, Indie is empathetic, and Eva is relaxed. Whenever someone is empathetic, they are clear-headed, so Indie being empathetic makes Indie clear-headed. If a person is inventive, then they are courageous; thus any inventive Eva would be courageous. A relaxed person is automatically focused, which means Eva, being relaxed, is also focused. Logical people are motivated, so any logical Indie would be motivated. Generous individuals are talented, and generous Val would therefore be talented. Determined people are wise, so Val's determination gives Val wisdom. If someone is studious, then they cannot be clear-headed; therefore a studious Indie would not be clear-headed. The knowledge base also contains three alternatives: either Indie is studious or Indie is logical; either Eva is not focused or Eva is inventive; and either Val is not wise or Val is generous. No other defaults apply.
examples.question: Is Indie motivated?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Indie possesses the motivated property. The goal is to trace a chain of implications from the known facts and rules to reach a statement about Indie’s motivation.

The first premise gives us a direct conditional: any entity that is empathetic is also clear-headed. This will become useful once we know Indie is empathetic. It sets up a bridge from empathy to clear-headedness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that empathetic Indie implies clear-headed Indie is introduced.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(indie) :- empathetic(indie)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that logical individuals are motivated. If we can show Indie is logical, this rule will immediately give us the desired motivated conclusion. It is a crucial link in the reasoning chain.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that logical Indie implies motivated Indie is introduced.</text>
<logic>motivated(indie) :- logical(indie)</logic>
</step>

The third premise provides a negative implication: if someone is studious, they cannot be clear-headed. This will help us eliminate the possibility that Indie is studious once we have evidence of Indie being clear-headed.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that studious Indie implies not clear-headed Indie is introduced.</text>
<logic>~clear_headed(indie) :- studious(indie)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise offers a choice: Indie is either studious or logical. This disjunction means that if we can rule out one alternative, the other must hold. It creates a point where we can apply reasoning by elimination.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>A disjunction stating that Indie is either studious or logical is presented.</text>
<logic>studious(indie) v logical(indie)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise states the fact that Indie is empathetic. Combined with Premise 1, this fact allows us to infer something concrete about Indie’s mental state, namely clear-headedness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Indie is empathetic is stated.</text>
<logic>empathetic(indie)</logic>
</step>

Because Indie is empathetic (Premise 5) and the rule says empathy guarantees clear-headedness (Premise 1), we can directly conclude that Indie is clear-headed. This step solidifies the positive attribute needed for the next elimination.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Indie being empathetic and the rule linking empathy to clear-headedness, we infer that Indie is clear-headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(indie)</logic>
</step>

We now have Indie being clear-headed, and Premise 3 says a studious Indie would be not clear-headed. Since Indie is indeed clear-headed, the only way for both statements to be true is if Indie is not studious. Thus we derive the negation of the studious alternative.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Using the fact that Indie is clear-headed together with the rule that studiousness leads to not clear-headedness, we conclude Indie cannot be studious.</text>
<logic>~studious(indie)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction from Premise 4 says Indie is either studious or logical. Having established that Indie cannot be studious (Step 7), the only remaining possibility is that Indie must be logical. This uses the principle of disjunctive syllogism to lock in logicalness.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given that Indie is not studious and the disjunction that Indie is either studious or logical, we deduce Indie must be logical.</text>
<logic>logical(indie)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Indie is logical, Premise 2 tells us that any logical individual is motivated. Applying this rule yields the conclusion that Indie is motivated, achieving the target property.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since Indie is logical and logicalness leads to motivation, we infer that Indie is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(indie)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Indie is motivated, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the pottery studio, Orion, Riley, and Rowan are all known to be evidence‑driven craftsmen. Whenever a potter is evidence‑driven, that person is considered fit for the upcoming exhibition, and specifically for Rowan this means that if Rowan is evidence‑driven then Rowan is fit. Moreover, any potter who is fit becomes resilient, so if Rowan is fit then Rowan is resilient. If a potter is resilient, they are regarded as humble, hence Rowan being resilient guarantees that Rowan is humble. Finally, humility leads to rational decision‑making; thus whenever Rowan is humble, Rowan is rational.
examples.question: Is it the case that Rowan is not rational?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement "Rowan is not rational" holds. To evaluate its truth we will try to derive the positive property rational(rowan) from the given facts and rules, because showing rational(rowan) would directly falsify its negation.

The first rule tells us that evidence‑driven status leads to being fit for Rowan. This connection will be useful later, because we already know Rowan is evidence‑driven, giving us a pathway to other attributes.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Rowan is evidence‑driven, she is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(rowan) :- evidence_driven(rowan)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links resilience to humility for Rowan. Once we obtain resilience, we can immediately obtain humility, moving us toward rationality.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Rowan is resilient, then she is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(rowan) :- resilient(rowan)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that humility guarantees rationality. This is the final step needed to establish the target property, so we must aim to prove humility first.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Rowan is humble, then she is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(rowan) :- humble(rowan)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule creates a bridge from being fit to being resilient. It lets us continue the chain that started with evidence‑driven, moving from fit to resilience.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Rowan is fit, then she is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(rowan) :- fit(rowan)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Rowan is evidence‑driven. This fact activates the first rule and starts the derivation chain.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Rowan is evidence‑driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(rowan)</logic>
</step>

Using the evidence‑driven fact together with the rule that evidence‑driven implies fit, we can apply modus ponens to conclude that Rowan is fit. This gives us the first intermediate property needed for the chain.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Because Rowan is evidence‑driven and the rule linking evidence‑driven to fit, we infer that Rowan is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(rowan)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have fit(rowan), the rule fit → resilient allows us to infer that Rowan is resilient. This step extends the chain from fit toward humility.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>From Rowan’s newly established fit status and the rule that fit implies resilient, we conclude that Rowan is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(rowan)</logic>
</step>

With resilient(rowan) established, the rule resilient → humble lets us deduce humility for Rowan. At this point we have reached the premise required for the final rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Since Rowan is resilient, the rule that resilient leads to humble lets us deduce that Rowan is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(rowan)</logic>
</step>

Since Rowan is humble, the rule humble → rational lets us conclude that Rowan is rational. This directly establishes the positive counterpart of the query statement.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Given Rowan is humble, the rule that humble implies rational allows us to infer that Rowan is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(rowan)</logic>
</step>

We derived Rowan is rational, so the conclusion Rowan is not rational is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the current economic report the market_sentiment is described as investor_confident and its data_prepared status is already verified. Whenever a sentiment's data_prepared condition holds, the analysis declares that the sentiment's trend_confirmed. Moreover, any sentiment that is trend_confirmed and for which there is no indication of abnormal inactivity (that is, not ab_active) is regarded as trading_active. No observation of abnormal inactivity has been recorded for the market_sentiment, so the default condition is satisfied.
examples.question: Is the market_sentiment trading_active?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the market_sentiment can be considered trading_active in the economic scenario. The goal is to trace the given facts and rules to see if the conditions for trading_active are satisfied.

This premise introduces a direct implication: prepared data guarantees a confirmed trend. Recognizing this link is essential because it will allow us to move from the known prepared fact to the needed confirmed condition.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if a sentiment's data is prepared, then its trend becomes confirmed.</text>
<logic>trend_confirmed(market_sentiment) :- data_prepared(market_sentiment)</logic>
</step>

The default rule provides the pathway from a confirmed trend to an active trading status, but only if there is no sign of abnormal inactivity. Highlighting the absence of ab_active is important for later applying this rule.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The default rule states that a sentiment that is trend_confirmed and not ab_active will be trading_active.</text>
<logic>trading_active(market_sentiment) :- trend_confirmed(market_sentiment), not ab_trading_active(market_sentiment)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the concrete fact that the market_sentiment's data is prepared. This fact will serve as the starting point for applying the implication in PREMISE_1.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The market_sentiment's data has been prepared.</text>
<logic>data_prepared(market_sentiment)</logic>
</step>

By combining the prepared fact (PREMISE_3) with the implication that preparation leads to confirmation (PREMISE_1), we infer that the market_sentiment's trend is indeed confirmed. This step bridges the gap between the known data and the condition needed for the default rule.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Because the data is prepared and the rule links preparation to confirmation, we can conclude that the market_sentiment's trend is confirmed.</text>
<logic>trend_confirmed(market_sentiment)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the trend is confirmed (STEP_4) and we know the report mentions no abnormal inactivity, the conditions of the default rule (PREMISE_2) are met. Consequently, we can safely conclude that the market_sentiment is trading_active.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the trend is confirmed and there is no evidence of abnormal inactivity, the default rule lets us infer that the market_sentiment is trading_active.</text>
<logic>trading_active(market_sentiment)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Market_sentiment is trading active, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The shutter follows the rule of thirds. The aperture is stabilized. Any shutter that follows the rule of thirds is also sharp. Whenever a shutter is exposed, it is bold. If an aperture is stabilized, then it is well exposed. Being well exposed guarantees that the aperture is soft. Either the shutter is not sharp or it is exposed. By default, a soft aperture becomes focused unless there is an abnormality that blocks focus.
examples.question: Is the aperture not focused?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the aperture is not focused. To answer this, we will use the given facts about the aperture’s state and the photographic rules that connect those states.

The rule linking stabilization to being well exposed is essential because it starts the chain that will eventually lead to a conclusion about focus. Recognizing that a stabilized aperture is automatically well exposed gives us a foothold for further inference.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If an aperture is stabilized, then it is well exposed.</text>
<logic>well_exposed(aperture) :- stabilized(aperture)</logic>
</step>

The next conditional tells us that once something is well exposed, it becomes soft. This connection is needed to move from the well‑exposed property we will obtain to the softness required by the default focus rule.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being well exposed guarantees that the aperture is soft.</text>
<logic>soft(aperture) :- well_exposed(aperture)</logic>
</step>

The default rule captures the usual behavior of a soft aperture: it will be focused unless something abnormal prevents it. This rule is the only way we can derive a statement about focus from softness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>By default, a soft aperture becomes focused unless an abnormality blocks it.</text>
<logic>focused(aperture) :- soft(aperture), not ab_focused(aperture)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the aperture is stabilized is our starting point; it supplies the concrete information required to trigger the conditional chain described in PREMISE_1.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The aperture is stabilized.</text>
<logic>stabilized(aperture)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that the aperture is stabilized and the rule that stabilization implies being well exposed, we can directly conclude that the aperture is well exposed. This step bridges the initial fact to the next conditional in the chain.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since the aperture is stabilized and stabilization implies being well exposed, we infer that the aperture is well exposed.</text>
<logic>well_exposed(aperture)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the aperture is well exposed, we apply the rule that well exposure guarantees softness, allowing us to infer that the aperture is soft. This soft state is precisely what the default focus rule needs.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Because the aperture is well exposed and well exposure leads to softness, we conclude the aperture is soft.</text>
<logic>soft(aperture)</logic>
</step>

With the aperture now known to be soft, and assuming no abnormality blocks focus, the default rule activates, letting us infer that the aperture is focused. This step completes the forward reasoning from the original fact to a focus conclusion.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given that the aperture is soft and there is no abnormality preventing focus, the default rule lets us infer that the aperture is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(aperture)</logic>
</step>

We derived Aperture is focused, so the conclusion Aperture is not focused is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is Fighter_chen swift?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several properties about three martial artists and a handful of conditional statements linking those properties. Our goal is to determine whether Fighter_chen possesses the property of being swift, which corresponds to the predicate efficient in the original formulation. To do this we will use the facts and rules provided, chaining them together logically.

The first rule tells us that lacking effectiveness guarantees swiftness. This is directly relevant because if we can show Fighter_chen is not effective, the rule will let us conclude he is swift. We keep this implication in mind for later steps.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Fighter_chen is not effective, then he is swift.</text>
<logic>swift(fighter_chen) :- ~effective(fighter_chen)</logic>
</step>

The second rule provides the converse conditional: if Fighter_chen were effective, he would not be conditioned. This will help us reason about the possibility of him being effective by contrasting it with the known fact that he is conditioned.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Fighter_chen is effective, then he is not conditioned.</text>
<logic>~conditioned(fighter_chen) :- effective(fighter_chen)</logic>
</step>

<logic>conditioned(fighter_chen)</logic>
</step>

We know from the fact that Fighter_chen is conditioned. The second rule says that effectiveness would force him to be not conditioned, which contradicts the fact. Therefore effectiveness cannot hold, and we can assert that Fighter_chen is not effective. This use of Modus Tollens bridges the fact and the conditional to produce the negated conclusion.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Since Fighter_chen is conditioned and being effective would make him not conditioned, we infer that Fighter_chen cannot be effective; therefore he is not effective.</text>
<logic>~effective(fighter_chen)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Fighter_chen is not effective, we can now apply the first rule, which states that not being effective leads to swiftness. By Modus Ponens, the antecedent of the rule is satisfied, so we deduce that Fighter_chen is swift. This completes the chain needed to answer the query.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Because Fighter_chen is not effective and the rule says that not effective implies swift, we conclude that Fighter_chen is swift.</text>
<logic>swift(fighter_chen)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Fighter_chen is swift, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is it true that master_seth is not quick?

<think>
We are asked to determine whether master_seth fails to be quick. To answer this, we must trace the chain of properties that connect the given facts to the attribute of quickness.

The first rule tells us that being studious guarantees mindfulness. This connection will let us turn the known fact about master_seth's studiousness into a statement about his mindfulness, which is needed for later reasoning.

The third rule states that encouraging behavior leads to a disciplined mindset. This will be useful once we establish that master_seth is encouraging, allowing us to move toward readiness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any encouraging martial artist is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(master_seth) :- encouraging(master_seth)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that discipline results in being ready. This provides the final bridge from the earlier properties to the readiness condition required by premise 2.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any disciplined martial artist is ready.</text>
<logic>ready(master_seth) :- disciplined(master_seth)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a logical alternative: either master_seth is not mindful or he is encouraging. Knowing the truth value of one side will let us determine the other.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The statement that either master_seth is not mindful or he is encouraging.</text>
<logic>~mindful(master_seth) v encouraging(master_seth)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that master_seth is studious. This concrete fact is the starting point for applying the first rule and building the chain of deductions.

<logic>studious(master_seth)</logic>
</step>

Applying Modus Ponens to the fact that master_seth is studious and the rule that studious implies mindful, we derive that master_seth is mindful. This establishes the left side of the disjunction as true.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">

<logic>mindful(master_seth)</logic>
</step>

With master_seth confirmed as mindful, the disjunctive statement "not mindful or encouraging" cannot rely on the first option, so the second option must hold. Hence, master_seth is encouraging.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Because master_seth is mindful, the alternative ‘not mindful’ in the disjunction is false, so we conclude that master_seth is encouraging.</text>
<logic>encouraging(master_seth)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have master_seth as encouraging, we use the rule that encouraging leads to disciplined. Modus Ponens gives us that master_seth is disciplined, moving us one step closer to readiness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Given that master_seth is encouraging and the rule that encouraging implies disciplined, we deduce that master_seth is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(master_seth)</logic>
</step>

From the disciplined status and the rule that disciplined implies ready, we infer via Modus Ponens that master_seth is ready. This satisfies the antecedent needed for the quickness rule.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since master_seth is disciplined and disciplined leads to ready, we obtain that master_seth is ready.</text>
<logic>ready(master_seth)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the rule that readiness guarantees quickness, we apply Modus Ponens to obtain that master_seth is quick. This directly contradicts the claim that he is not quick.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Having established that master_seth is ready, and that ready implies quick, we conclude that master_seth is quick.</text>
<logic>quick(master_seth)</logic>
</step>

We derived Master_seth is quick, so the conclusion Master_seth is not quick is False.
</think>
Answer: False

At least one of the following holds: Zara is brilliant or Zara is reliable. Also, either Tate is not motivated or Tate is creative. These statements constitute all the facts and rules we will use.
examples.question: Is Zara proficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Zara possesses the property of being proficient. To answer, we will follow the chain of implications and the given disjunctions, starting from the facts that are directly stated.

The rule linking punctuality to agility is essential because later steps will need to move from punctual Zara to agile Zara. Recognizing this conditional helps us see how a later fact about reliability can eventually produce agility.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is punctual, then Zara is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(zara) :- punctual(zara)</logic>
</step>

The reliability‑to‑punctuality rule tells us that if we ever establish Zara's reliability, we can immediately obtain punctuality. This bridge is crucial for the forward reasoning that follows the disjunction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is reliable, then Zara is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(zara) :- reliable(zara)</logic>
</step>

The statement that agility leads to relaxation will allow us to connect the earlier derived agile property to a relaxed state. This connection is later used to test the proficiency rule that forbids relaxation.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is agile, then Zara is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(zara) :- agile(zara)</logic>
</step>

Strategic individuals are systematic, so Zara's strategic status directly yields systematic Zara. This premise will be the starting point for a contradiction involving brilliance.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If someone is strategic, then they are systematic; applied to Zara, strategic Zara makes her systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(zara) :- strategic(zara)</logic>
</step>

The proficiency rule says that if Zara were proficient, she could not be relaxed. This negative consequence will be used later to show that assuming proficiency leads to an impossible situation.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is proficient, then Zara is not relaxed.</text>
<logic>~relaxed(zara) :- proficient(zara)</logic>
</step>

The brilliance rule tells us that brilliant Zara would make her not systematic. This will be important when we derive that Zara is systematic, allowing us to rule out brilliance.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is brilliant, then Zara is not systematic.</text>
<logic>~systematic(zara) :- brilliant(zara)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction guarantees at least one of brilliant or reliable holds for Zara. Knowing this binary choice lets us later eliminate the brilliant option and therefore commit to reliability.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Zara is brilliant or Zara is reliable (or both).</text>
<logic>brilliant(zara) v reliable(zara)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Zara is strategic is the concrete piece of information we have about her. It activates the strategic‑to‑systematic rule and begins the chain of deductions.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Zara is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(zara)</logic>
</step>

Because Zara is strategic, the strategic‑to‑systematic rule applies, giving us systematic Zara. This step converts a known fact into a new property that will be pivotal for the next contradiction.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>From Zara being strategic we infer that Zara is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(zara)</logic>
</step>

Having systematic Zara and the rule that brilliant implies not systematic, we see that if Zara were brilliant, she would have to be not systematic, which clashes with the systematic fact we just derived. Therefore, brilliant Zara cannot be true, so we infer ~brilliant(zara).
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Because Zara is systematic, we can conclude that Zara is not brilliant.</text>
<logic>~brilliant(zara)</logic>
</step>

Now we have ~brilliant(zara) together with the disjunction that says brilliant or reliable must hold. Since brilliant is false, the only way for the disjunction to be satisfied is for reliable(zara) to be true, so we conclude Zara is reliable.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">
<text>Since Zara is not brilliant and we know that either brilliant or reliable holds, we deduce that Zara is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(zara)</logic>
</step>

Reliable Zara triggers the reliability‑to‑punctuality rule, allowing us to deduce punctual Zara. This step moves us forward along the chain that started with the disjunction.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Reliable Zara gives us punctual Zara.</text>
<logic>punctual(zara)</logic>
</step>

With punctual Zara established, the punctual‑to‑agile rule gives us agile Zara. This new agile property will later be used to reach a relaxed state.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Punctual Zara gives us agile Zara.</text>
<logic>agile(zara)</logic>
</step>

Agile Zara now activates the agility‑to‑relaxation rule, producing relaxed Zara. We now have a direct conflict with the condition that a proficient Zara must be not relaxed.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">
<text>Agile Zara gives us relaxed Zara.</text>
<logic>relaxed(zara)</logic>
</step>

Since we have relaxed Zara and the rule that proficient Zara would imply not relaxed, we see that assuming proficiency leads to a contradiction. Therefore, we must conclude that Zara cannot be proficient, i.e., ~proficient(zara).
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Relaxed Zara together with the rule that proficient implies not relaxed lets us infer that Zara cannot be proficient.</text>
<logic>~proficient(zara)</logic>
</step>

We derived Zara is not proficient, so the conclusion Zara is proficient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

The disjunction says that either router_jill is not latency_sensitive or she performs packet_forwarding. This provides a bridge between her latency sensitivity and packet forwarding.

<text>The statement that router_jill is either not latency_sensitive or it performs packet_forwarding.</text>
<logic>~latency_sensitive(router_jill) v packet_forwarding(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

We are told explicitly that router_jill is latency_sensitive. This fact will allow us to eliminate the first part of the disjunction.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact that router_jill is latency_sensitive.</text>
<logic>latency_sensitive(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Jill is latency_sensitive, the alternative 'not latency_sensitive' in the disjunction cannot hold. Therefore the remaining option must be true, giving us that Jill performs packet_forwarding.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Since router_jill is latency_sensitive, the alternative 'not latency_sensitive' is false, so the disjunction forces that router_jill performs packet_forwarding.</text>
<logic>packet_forwarding(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Jill performs packet_forwarding and using the rule that forwarding implies dynamic_routing, we can directly infer that Jill performs dynamic_routing.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Because router_jill performs packet_forwarding and any router that does so also performs dynamic_routing, we infer that router_jill performs dynamic_routing.</text>
<logic>dynamic_routing(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

We now have that Jill performs dynamic_routing. The rule that a secure_node would not perform dynamic_routing gives us the contrapositive: if a router does perform dynamic_routing, it cannot be secure_node. Hence we deduce that Jill is not a secure_node.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Router_jill performs dynamic_routing, and a secure_node would not perform dynamic_routing; therefore router_jill cannot be a secure_node.</text>
<logic>~secure_node(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Router_jill is not secure node, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the ancient city, Luca is known to be disciplined, and Cora is noted for her politeness. Whenever someone is disciplined, that person is also agile, so Luca's discipline makes him agile. If a person is compassionate, then that person is rational; thus compassion guarantees rationality. Should a person be vigorous, that person cannot be rational, establishing that vigor leads to irrationality. Talent brings systematic skill, so any talented individual is systematic. Regarding possibilities, either Luca is not agile or Luca is talented, and either Cora is not polite or Cora is compassionate. No other default assumptions are made.
examples.question: Is Cora vigorous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the ancient figure Cora possesses the quality of vigor. To answer, we will trace the logical consequences of the facts and rules provided about Cora's character traits.

The rule linking compassion to rationality is crucial because it allows us to turn any evidence of compassion into evidence of rationality. Recognizing this conditional helps us later when we establish Cora's compassion.

<logic>rational(cora) :- compassionate(cora)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that vigor would contradict rationality; it sets up a direct conflict between being vigorous and being rational. This will be used to negate vigor once we know Cora is rational.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Cora were vigorous, then she would not be rational.</text>
<logic>~rational(cora) :- vigorous(cora)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives concerning Cora's politeness and compassion. It will interact with the known fact of her politeness to force the other alternative, namely compassion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Cora is not polite or Cora is compassionate.</text>
<logic>~polite(cora) v compassionate(cora)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Cora is polite, which directly engages with the disjunction from the previous premise. This fact is the starting point for applying disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Cora is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(cora)</logic>
</step>

Because Cora is polite, the first part of the disjunction (~polite) is false, leaving the only viable option that Cora must be compassionate. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism, turning the disjunction into a definite statement.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because Cora is polite and either she is not polite or compassionate, we must conclude that Cora is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(cora)</logic>
</step>

Having established Cora's compassion, we can now invoke the earlier rule that compassion guarantees rationality. Thus, Cora inherits the property of being rational from her compassionate state.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Cora is compassionate and compassion implies rationality, we infer that Cora is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(cora)</logic>
</step>

We now have Cora as rational, but the rule about vigor says that if she were vigorous, she would not be rational. Since she is rational, the antecedent of that rule (vigorous) must be false, leading to the conclusion that Cora is not vigorous.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that Cora is rational, and that vigor would make her not rational, we deduce that Cora cannot be vigorous.</text>
<logic>~vigorous(cora)</logic>

We derived Cora is not vigorous, so the conclusion Cora is vigorous is False.

examples.context: In the chess‑strategy world, Emerson is known to be courageous, and Miles is also courageous, optimistic, and sharp. Whenever someone is courageous, that person is efficient. Whenever someone is sharp, that person is honest. If a person is industrious then that person is impartial. If a person is optimistic then that person is industrious. If a person is honest then that person is motivated. If a person is wise then that person is not efficient. Either Emerson is wise or Emerson is sharp. Either Emerson is not motivated or Emerson is optimistic.
examples.question: Is it the case that Emerson is not impartial?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Emerson fails to be impartial. To answer this, we need to determine if the premises inevitably lead to Emerson being impartial. If we can derive impartial(emerson), then the negated statement will be false.

The rule that courageous implies efficient is relevant because we already know Emerson is courageous; this connection will let us produce efficiency later on. It sets up a chain toward other properties.
<step n="1" type="premise">
