</step>

Sharpness implying honesty will become useful once we can establish that Emerson is sharp. It provides a route from a sensory quality to a moral quality needed for later steps.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being sharp guarantees honesty.</text>
<logic>honest(emerson) :- sharp(emerson)</logic>
</step>

The link from industriousness to impartiality is the crucial bridge to the final goal; once we obtain industrious(emerson), we can directly infer impartial(emerson).
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>All industrious people are impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(emerson) :- industrious(emerson)</logic>
</step>

Optimism leading to industriousness gives us a way to reach impartiality via the previous rule. If we can show Emerson is optimistic, we will get industriousness automatically.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Optimism leads to industriousness.</text>
<logic>industrious(emerson) :- optimistic(emerson)</logic>
</step>

Honesty causing motivation will later allow us to use the disjunction involving motivation and optimism. It creates a forward path from honesty to a property that appears in a disjunctive premise.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Honesty causes motivation.</text>
<logic>motivated(emerson) :- honest(emerson)</logic>
</step>

The rule that wisdom blocks efficiency will be instrumental in eliminating the possibility that Emerson is wise, which, together with a disjunction, will force him to be sharp. This negative information is needed to activate the sharp‑honest link.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If someone is wise they cannot be efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(emerson) :- wise(emerson)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "wise or sharp" tells us at least one of those holds for Emerson. Combined with the previous premise that he cannot be wise, we will be forced to accept that he is sharp.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Emerson is either wise or sharp.</text>
<logic>wise(emerson) v sharp(emerson)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction gives a relationship between motivation and optimism. Once we have motivation, this clause will let us infer optimism, which then unlocks industriousness.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Emerson lacks motivation or he is optimistic.</text>
<logic>~motivated(emerson) v optimistic(emerson)</logic>
</step>

Stating that Emerson is courageous gives us the starting fact required to trigger the efficiency rule. This fact anchors the whole deduction chain.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Emerson possesses courage.</text>
<logic>courageous(emerson)</logic>
</step>

Using the courageous fact together with the rule that courage implies efficiency, we can directly conclude that Emerson is efficient. This is a straightforward application of the first premise.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>From Emerson's courage we infer that Emerson is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(emerson)</logic>
</step>

We now have efficient(emerson). The rule that a wise person cannot be efficient tells us that if Emerson were wise, he would not be efficient, which contradicts what we just derived. Therefore, Emerson must not be wise. This eliminates the wise alternative in the disjunction.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Because Emerson is efficient and wisdom would block efficiency, we deduce that Emerson cannot be wise.</text>
<logic>~wise(emerson)</logic>
</step>

Since we have shown Emerson is not wise and the disjunction asserts that he is either wise or sharp, the only viable option left is that Emerson is sharp. Thus we derive sharp(emerson).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,7">
<text>Since Emerson is not wise and he must be either wise or sharp, we conclude that Emerson is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(emerson)</logic>
</step>

With sharp(emerson) established, the rule linking sharpness to honesty lets us infer that Emerson is honest. This moves us further along the chain toward motivation.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Sharpness gives us honesty, so Emerson is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(emerson)</logic>
</step>

Having honest(emerson) and the rule that honesty leads to motivation enables us to conclude motivated(emerson). This new fact will be useful for the next disjunctive inference.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">
<text>Honesty yields motivation, therefore Emerson is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(emerson)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that either Emerson is not motivated or he is optimistic. Because we now know he is motivated, the first part is false, so the second part must hold; consequently Emerson is optimistic.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,8">
<text>Motivation together with the disjunction that either Emerson is not motivated or optimistic forces Emerson to be optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(emerson)</logic>
</step>

Optimistic(emerson) triggers the rule that optimism yields industriousness, giving us industrious(emerson). This is the key property needed to reach impartiality.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,4">

<logic>industrious(emerson)</logic>

Finally, the rule that every industrious individual is impartial allows us to conclude impartial(emerson) from the industrious fact we just obtained.

We derived Emerson is impartial, so the conclusion Emerson is not impartial is False.

examples.context: Micah is punctual. Whenever something is punctual, it is optimistic, so Micah being punctual guarantees that Micah is optimistic. If Micah is optimistic, then Micah is empathetic; being optimistic leads to empathy. Any empathetic person is also efficient, therefore if Micah is empathetic, Micah must be efficient. If Micah is compassionate, then Micah is not efficient, i.e., compassion blocks efficiency. Being agile makes one resourceful, so any agile individual is automatically resourceful. Finally, either Micah is compassionate or Micah is agile (or possibly both).
examples.question: Can we conclude that Micah is not resourceful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are presented with a collection of facts and conditional statements about Micah's personal qualities. Our task is to see whether these premises force us to accept the claim that Micah is not resourceful.

This premise connects empathy to efficiency, establishing a chain we will later use to move from emotional traits to a performance trait. Recognizing this link is crucial for later steps where we will derive efficiency from earlier properties.

<text>The rule says that if Micah is empathetic then Micah is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(micah) :- empathetic(micah)</logic>
</step>

Here we are told that optimism guarantees empathy. By linking optimism to empathy, this rule lets us translate an optimistic state into an empathetic one, which then feeds into the efficiency rule.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Micah is optimistic then Micah is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(micah) :- optimistic(micah)</logic>
</step>

The punctuality‑to‑optimism rule gives us a starting point: from the known fact of punctuality we can obtain optimism. This creates the first domino in the chain that will eventually lead to efficiency.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Micah is punctual then Micah is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(micah) :- punctual(micah)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that agility is sufficient for resourcefulness. It will become important after we figure out whether Micah is agile, because it will allow us to infer resourcefulness directly.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Micah is agile then Micah is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(micah) :- agile(micah)</logic>
</step>

The compassionate‑to‑not‑efficient rule provides a way to block efficiency if compassion holds. It will later enable us to reason about the impossibility of compassion once we have established efficiency.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Micah is compassionate then Micah is not efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(micah) :- compassionate(micah)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that Micah must be either compassionate or agile (or both). This binary choice will be resolved once we have information about compassion, allowing us to deduce agility if compassion is ruled out.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The premise states that either Micah is compassionate or Micah is agile.</text>
<logic>compassionate(micah) v agile(micah)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Micah is punctual is the only concrete piece of information given outright. It serves as the anchor for the forward‑chaining reasoning that follows.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is a given fact that Micah is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(micah)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Micah is punctual together with the rule that punctuality implies optimism, we can confidently assert that Micah is optimistic. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens: the antecedent (punctual) is true, so the consequent (optimistic) must be true.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>From Micah being punctual and the rule linking punctuality to optimism, we infer that Micah is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(micah)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Micah's optimism, we apply the rule linking optimism to empathy. Since the antecedent (optimistic) holds, we infer the consequent (empathetic), again via modus ponens. This moves us one step closer to efficiency.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Using Micah's optimism together with the rule that optimism implies empathy, we deduce that Micah is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(micah)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that Micah is empathetic, we invoke the empathy‑to‑efficiency rule. The true antecedent (empathetic) triggers the consequent, giving us that Micah is efficient. This completes the forward chain from punctuality to efficiency.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Since Micah is empathetic and the rule says empathy leads to efficiency, we conclude that Micah is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(micah)</logic>
</step>

At this point we possess both that Micah is efficient and the rule that compassion would make him not efficient. By contraposition (modus tollens), if Micah were compassionate, he would have to be not efficient, which contradicts the established efficiency. Therefore, Micah cannot be compassionate.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Because Micah is efficient and the rule says compassion would make him not efficient, we can infer that Micah cannot be compassionate.</text>
<logic>~compassionate(micah)</logic>

With the knowledge that Micah is not compassionate, the earlier disjunction (compassionate or agile) can be resolved: the compassionate side is false, so the only remaining possibility is that Micah is agile. This is an instance of disjunctive syllogism, eliminating the false disjunct.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given that Micah is not compassionate and the disjunction that he is either compassionate or agile, we eliminate the compassionate option and therefore infer that Micah is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(micah)</logic>

Now that we have agility for Micah, the rule stating that agility implies resourcefulness allows us to conclude that Micah is resourceful. This follows directly by modus ponens, using the true antecedent (agile).
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>From Micah being agile and the rule that agility guarantees resourcefulness, we infer that Micah is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(micah)</logic>
</step>

We derived Micah is resourceful, so the conclusion Micah is not resourceful is False.

examples.question: Is Dawn attentive?

We are asked to decide whether Dawn possesses the quality of attentiveness. The goal is to trace a chain of logical implications starting from the facts we know about Dawn and the rules that connect those facts to attentiveness.

The fourth premise presents a disjunction: either Dawn is not athletic or Dawn is thorough. This alternative will be instrumental after we have determined Dawn's athletic status, because it forces the conclusion that she must be thorough.
<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>~athletic(dawn) v thorough(dawn)</logic>

We are explicitly told that Dawn is systematic. This factual statement is the starting point of our deduction chain, providing the concrete evidence required to activate the first conditional rule.

<logic>systematic(dawn)</logic>
</step>

Because Dawn is systematic (from PREMISE_5) and the rule in PREMISE_1 says systematic implies ambitious, we can conclude Dawn is ambitious. This application of the conditional is straightforward and gives us a new fact to work with.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Dawn being systematic and the first rule, we infer that Dawn is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(dawn)</logic>

Now that we have established Dawn's ambition, we can invoke the rule from PREMISE_2, which says ambition leads to athleticism. Consequently, Dawn is athletic, extending our chain of derived properties.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Using Dawn's newly established ambition and the second rule, we conclude Dawn is athletic.</text>

Having derived that Dawn is athletic, we turn to the disjunction in PREMISE_4. Since the first part of the disjunction (~athletic) is false (Dawn is athletic), the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second part, thorough, to be true. Therefore, Dawn must be thorough.

With Dawn's thoroughness now confirmed, the rule from PREMISE_3 applies, turning thoroughness into attentiveness. This gives us the desired conclusion that Dawn is attentive.

<text>Since Dawn is thorough, the third rule lets us infer that Dawn is attentive.</text>

Therefore Dawn is attentive, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: In the forensic team, James, Rex, and Willow are all noted for their empathy; each of them is empathetic. The investigators follow a rule that whenever James is empathetic, he is also respectful. Moreover, any time James is respectful, the team considers him capable. Conversely, if James were brave, that would guarantee that he is not capable. These statements constitute all the facts and conditional relationships relevant to the case.
examples.question: Is it true that James is not brave?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether James lacks bravery. The goal is to derive the negation of the predicate brave applied to James using the supplied facts and rules.

This premise connects respect to capability for James, which will later allow us to obtain a statement about his capability once we know he is respectful. It sets up a forward link that is essential for the chain of inference.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if James is respectful, then he is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(james) :- respectful(james)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a conditional linking empathy to respect, showing that James's empathy guarantees his respectfulness. This rule will let us move from the known fact of empathy to the needed intermediate property of respect.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if James is empathetic, then he is respectful.</text>
<logic>respectful(james) :- empathetic(james)</logic>
</step>

This rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if James were brave, he would necessarily be not capable. It will be used later to argue that James cannot be brave because we will have evidence that he is capable.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if James is brave, then he is not capable.</text>
<logic>~capable(james) :- brave(james)</logic>
</step>

The fact that James is empathetic is our starting point; it is explicitly stated in the context and will trigger the rule from PREMISE_2. Without this fact, we could not initiate the reasoning chain.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that James is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(james)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from PREMISE_2 to the fact in PREMISE_4, we see that James's empathy forces him to be respectful. This step translates the known empathy into the required respect property, preparing us for the next inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From James being empathetic and the rule that empathy leads to respect, we infer that James is respectful.</text>
<logic>respectful(james)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established James is respectful, we can invoke PREMISE_1, which tells us that any respectful James is capable. Hence we deduce that James is capable, moving us closer to a contradiction with the bravery rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since James is respectful and the rule linking respect to capability, we conclude that James is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(james)</logic>
</step>

We now possess the statement that James is capable. PREMISE_3 tells us that bravery would imply not capable, which is the opposite of what we have. By modus tollens, if the consequent (~capable) is false (because James is capable), the antecedent (brave) must also be false. Thus James cannot be brave.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given that James is capable and the rule that bravery would make him not capable, we deduce that James cannot be brave.</text>
<logic>~brave(james)</logic>
</step>

Therefore James is not brave, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The master carpenter is courteous. Whenever the master carpenter is not stocked, he becomes nimble. If the master carpenter is courteous, then he is strong. If the master carpenter is stocked, then he is not inventive. Either the master carpenter is not strong, or he is inventive.
examples.question: Is the master carpenter nimble?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are told several properties about the master carpenter and how those properties relate to each other. Our goal is to determine whether the master carpenter can be described as nimble.

The first rule tells us that lacking the stocked condition guarantees nimbleness. This will be useful later once we can establish that the carpenter is not stocked. It sets up a direct link from a negative condition to the property we care about.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the master carpenter is not stocked, then he is nimble.</text>
<logic>nimble(master_carpenter) :- ~stocked(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects courteousness to strength. Since we already know the carpenter is courteous, this rule will let us derive that he is strong, which will be needed for the disjunctive reasoning that follows.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the master carpenter is courteous, then he is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(master_carpenter) :- courteous(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that being stocked forces the carpenter to be not inventive. This contrapositive relationship will later let us infer the absence of the stocked condition from the presence of inventiveness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the master carpenter is stocked, then he is not inventive.</text>
<logic>~inventive(master_carpenter) :- stocked(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice: either the carpenter lacks strength or he is inventive. Once we have information about his strength, we can resolve this clause and possibly obtain inventiveness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the master carpenter is not strong, or he is inventive.</text>
<logic>~strong(master_carpenter) v inventive(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the master carpenter is courteous is the starting point for the chain of deductions. It directly satisfies the antecedent of the courteous‑to‑strong rule.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The master carpenter is courteous.</text>
<logic>courteous(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

Because the carpenter is courteous and the rule says courteous implies strong, we can confidently assert that he is strong. This deduction supplies the positive side of the disjunction needed next.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From the fact that the master carpenter is courteous and the rule that courteous implies strong, we infer that the master carpenter is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that the carpenter is strong, the disjunction "not strong or inventive" cannot be satisfied by the first alternative, so the only way for the statement to hold is for the carpenter to be inventive. Thus we conclude inventiveness.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Since the master carpenter is strong, the disjunction that either he is not strong or inventive forces us to conclude that he must be inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

We now have that the carpenter is inventive. The earlier rule tells us that if he were stocked, he would be not inventive. Since he is indeed inventive, the stocked condition must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Hence, we deduce that he is not stocked.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Knowing the master carpenter is inventive and that being stocked would make him not inventive, we apply modus tollens to infer that he is not stocked.</text>
<logic>~stocked(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the carpenter is not stocked, we can apply the first rule, which says that not being stocked leads to nimbleness. Therefore, we conclude that the master carpenter is nimble.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Given that the master carpenter is not stocked, the rule that not stocked implies nimble allows us to conclude that he is nimble.</text>
<logic>nimble(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Master_carpenter is nimble, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The western_front is rapid. Whenever the western_front is rapid, it is observant. Whenever the western_front is observant, it is organized. Whenever the western_front is stable, it is accurate. If the western_front is preemptive then it is not accurate. Moreover, the western_front is either not organized or it is stable.
examples.question: Is the western_front preemptive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the western_front possesses the preemptive quality. To answer, we will chain together the given facts and conditional statements until we can either confirm or refute preemptiveness.

The first rule tells us that observance guarantees organization for the western_front. This will become useful once we can show the western_front is observant, allowing us to derive organization later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the western_front is observant, then it is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(western_front) :- observant(western_front)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links rapidness to observance, meaning that any rapid entity, such as the western_front, must also be observant. This connection is the bridge from the known fact to the first derived property.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the western_front is rapid, then it is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(western_front) :- rapid(western_front)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that stability ensures accuracy. When we eventually establish that the western_front is stable, we will be able to infer its accuracy, which is needed for the final contradiction.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the western_front is stable, then it is accurate.</text>
<logic>accurate(western_front) :- stable(western_front)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if something were preemptive, it would not be accurate. By later showing the western_front is accurate, we can use this rule to deny preemptiveness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the western_front is preemptive, then it is not accurate.</text>
<logic>~accurate(western_front) :- preemptive(western_front)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says the western_front is either not organized or stable. This will let us deduce stability once we learn the western_front is indeed organized, by eliminating the first alternative.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The western_front is either not organized or stable.</text>
<logic>~organized(western_front) v stable(western_front)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told the western_front is rapid. This fact is the starting point for applying the rapid‑to‑observant rule and launching the chain of deductions.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The western_front is rapid.</text>
<logic>rapid(western_front)</logic>
</step>

Because the western_front is rapid (Premise 6) and rapid implies observant (Premise 2), we can directly conclude that the western_front is observant. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens, turning a known condition into a new property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From the fact that the western_front is rapid and the rule linking rapid to observant, we infer that the western_front is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(western_front)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the western_front is observant (Step 7), we now apply the rule that observant leads to organized (Premise 1). By modus ponens again, the western_front must be organized.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Using the newly obtained observant property and the rule that observant implies organized, we infer that the western_front is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(western_front)</logic>
</step>

We now have that the western_front is organized (Step 8). The disjunction (Premise 5) tells us that either the western_front is not organized or it is stable. Since the first option is false, the only remaining possibility is that the western_front is stable. This follows by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Since the western_front is organized, the disjunction tells us that the other alternative must hold, so the western_front is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(western_front)</logic>
</step>

With stability confirmed (Step 9) and the rule that stability guarantees accuracy (Premise 3), we can infer that the western_front is accurate. This is another direct modus ponens step, converting stability into accuracy.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From the stability of the western_front and the rule that stability yields accuracy, we infer that the western_front is accurate.</text>
<logic>accurate(western_front)</logic>
</step>

We know the western_front is accurate (Step 10). The rule stating that preemptive implies not accurate (Premise 4) can be contrapositively used: if something is accurate, it cannot be preemptive. Thus we deduce that the western_front is not preemptive.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because the western_front is accurate, the rule that preemptive leads to not accurate forces us to conclude that the western_front cannot be preemptive.</text>
<logic>~preemptive(western_front)</logic>
</step>

We derived Western_front is not preemptive, so the conclusion Western_front is preemptive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Steve is known to be empathetic, and Phoebe is grateful. Whenever someone is active, that person is also brilliant, so if Steve were active, he would be brilliant. If Steve were thorough, then he would not be brilliant. Phoebe becomes friendly whenever she is grateful, and she becomes judicious whenever she is cautious. Regarding possibilities, Steve is either not empathetic or active, and Phoebe is either not friendly or cautious. These statements capture all the given facts, conditionals, and alternatives.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Steve is not thorough?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Steve can be shown to be not thorough. The information includes facts about Steve's empathy, several conditional rules, and a disjunction that links his empathy to activity. Our goal is to chain these premises together to reach the negation of thoroughness.

The first rule tells us that activity guarantees brilliance for Steve. This will become useful once we establish Steve's activity, allowing us to move from a property we can derive to a stronger conclusion about his brilliance.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Steve is active, then Steve is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(steve) :- active(steve)</logic>
</step>

The second rule gives a contrapositive relationship: if Steve were thorough, he would necessarily lack brilliance. This connects the target predicate (thorough) to a property we can later contradict, setting up a modus tollens step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Steve is thorough, then Steve is not brilliant.</text>
<logic>~brilliant(steve) :- thorough(steve)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of 'Steve is not empathetic' or 'Steve is active' holds. Since we already know Steve is empathetic, the first disjunct is false, forcing the second disjunct—Steve must be active. This logical move is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Steve is either not empathetic or active.</text>
<logic>~empathetic(steve) v active(steve)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Steve is empathetic directly opposes the 'not empathetic' part of the disjunction, giving us the leverage to eliminate that option and isolate activity as the remaining truth.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Steve is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(steve)</logic>
</step>

Applying disjunctive syllogism to the disjunction and the empathetic fact, we conclude that Steve is active. The reasoning is straightforward: one disjunct is false, so the other must be true, establishing the activity needed for the next inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because Steve is empathetic, the disjunction forces the other option, so Steve must be active.</text>
<logic>active(steve)</logic>
</step>

With Steve now known to be active, we invoke the conditional rule from PREMISE_1. Because activity implies brilliance, we can directly infer that Steve is brilliant. This is a direct application of modus ponens, moving from the antecedent to the consequent.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Steve is active and the rule says an active Steve is brilliant, we infer that Steve is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(steve)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Steve's brilliance, and we also have the rule that thoroughness would make Steve not brilliant. Since Steve is indeed brilliant, the only way to avoid contradiction is to deny that he is thorough. This is an instance of modus tollens: if thorough ⇒ not brilliant, but we have brilliant, so thorough must be false.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that Steve is brilliant and the rule says a thorough Steve would be not brilliant, we conclude Steve cannot be thorough.</text>
<logic>~thorough(steve)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Steve is not thorough, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Noel is athletic. Whenever something is athletic, it is also eloquent. If something is eloquent, then it is thorough. Being thorough guarantees that the thing is knowledgeable. If something is humble, then it is honest. Whenever something is motivated, it becomes humble. If something is proficient, then it is not knowledgeable. Either Noel is proficient or Noel is motivated.
examples.question: Is it true that Noel is not honest?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the claim that Noel is not honest holds. To answer, we must follow the chain of given facts, rules, and the disjunction to see what we can deduce about Noel's honesty.

The first rule tells us that athleticness guarantees eloquence. This will let us turn the known fact about Noel's athletic ability into a statement about his eloquence later on.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any athletic thing is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(noel) :- athletic(noel)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links humility to honesty. It will be useful once we manage to establish that Noel is humble, because then we can immediately infer honesty.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any humble thing is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(noel) :- humble(noel)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that motivation leads to humility. This creates a bridge from the disjunctive option "motivated" toward the honesty conclusion via humility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any motivated thing is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(noel) :- motivated(noel)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule converts thoroughness into knowledgeability. It will help us later when we have shown Noel is thorough, allowing us to claim he is knowledgeable.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any thorough thing is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(noel) :- thorough(noel)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule makes eloquence sufficient for thoroughness. Combined with the earlier rule, this creates a chain from athletic to eloquent to thorough.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any eloquent thing is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(noel) :- eloquent(noel)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule states that if Noel were proficient, he would be not knowledgeable. This negative consequence will be used to eliminate the possibility that Noel is proficient.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any proficient thing is not knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>~knowledgeable(noel) :- proficient(noel)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise gives a disjunction: either Noel is proficient or he is motivated. This either‑or statement will be resolved once we rule out one of the alternatives.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that Noel is either proficient or motivated.</text>
<logic>proficient(noel) v motivated(noel)</logic>
</step>

The eighth premise provides the concrete fact that Noel is athletic, the starting point for the forward reasoning.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Noel is athletic.</text>
<logic>athletic(noel)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from PREMISE_1 to the fact in PREMISE_8, we see that Noel's athleticism forces him to be eloquent. This is a direct use of modus ponens, turning the condition into a conclusion.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From Noel being athletic and the rule linking athletic to eloquent, we infer Noel is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(noel)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Noel is eloquent, we invoke the rule from PREMISE_5, which says eloquence guarantees thoroughness. Hence, we can assert that Noel is thorough.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Using Noel's eloquence and the rule that eloquent implies thorough, we conclude Noel is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(noel)</logic>
</step>

With Noel being thorough, the rule from PREMISE_4 lets us infer that he is knowledgeable. The chain athletic → eloquent → thorough → knowledgeable is now complete.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>With Noel being thorough and the rule that thorough implies knowledgeable, we deduce Noel is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(noel)</logic>
</step>

We have Noel knowledgeable, but PREMISE_6 tells us that if Noel were proficient, he would be not knowledgeable. Since both knowledgeability and its negation cannot hold simultaneously, we must reject the antecedent, concluding that Noel cannot be proficient.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Because Noel is knowledgeable and the rule that proficient would make him not knowledgeable, we derive that Noel cannot be proficient.</text>
<logic>~proficient(noel)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction in PREMISE_7 says Noel is either proficient or motivated. Because we have just shown he cannot be proficient, the only remaining option is that he is motivated. This follows by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Given that Noel is not proficient and the disjunction that he is either proficient or motivated, we infer that Noel must be motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(noel)</logic>
</step>

Given Noel is motivated, we apply the rule from PREMISE_3, which converts motivation into humility. Thus, Noel becomes humble.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">
<text>Since Noel is motivated and motivated leads to humble, we obtain that Noel is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(noel)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the rule from PREMISE_2, Noel's humility leads directly to honesty. We have now derived that Noel is honest.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,2">
<text>From Noel being humble and the rule that humble entails honest, we get that Noel is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(noel)</logic>
</step>

We derived Noel is honest, so the conclusion Noel is not honest is False.
</think>
Answer: False

If Felix is trustworthy, then Felix is honest. A default principle holds that any animal that is recognized and for which there is no abnormality flag against trustworthiness is considered trustworthy; thus recognized Felix would become trustworthy provided no ab_trustworthy flag exists. Another default principle says that any animal that is honest and lacks an abnormality flag against validation is validated.
examples.question: Is it true that Felix is not validated?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the statement that Felix is not validated holds. To answer, we must trace how the information about Felix's recognition, trustworthiness, and honesty leads to a conclusion about his validation status.

The rule linking trustworthiness to honesty tells us that any trustworthy animal must also be honest. This connection will become useful once we establish Felix's trustworthiness later in the proof.

<text>If Felix can be trusted, then Felix is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(felix) :- trustworthy(felix)</logic>
</step>

The default rule explains that a recognized animal becomes trustworthy as long as no abnormality flag (ab_trustworthy) blocks it. This gives us a pathway from the known fact of Felix's recognition to a potential trustworthiness conclusion.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Felix is recognized and there is no evidence against his trustworthiness, he is considered trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(felix) :- recognized(felix), not ab_trustworthy(felix)</logic>
</step>

Another default rule states that an honest animal, provided there is no abnormality flag (ab_validated), is validated. This rule will allow us to move from honesty to validation once we have established honesty for Felix.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Felix is honest and there is no evidence against his validation, then Felix is validated.</text>
<logic>validated(felix) :- honest(felix), not ab_validated(felix)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Felix is recognized supplies the starting point for applying the default trustworthiness rule. Without this fact, we could not trigger the chain that leads to validation.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Felix has been recognized.</text>
<logic>recognized(felix)</logic>
</step>

Using the recognized fact together with the default rule that recognized animals become trustworthy when no ab_trustworthy flag is present, we can safely infer that Felix is trustworthy. This step sets up the condition needed for the honesty rule.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From the fact that Felix is recognized and the default rule, we infer that Felix is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(felix)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Felix is trustworthy, the earlier rule that trustworthiness implies honesty lets us conclude that Felix is honest. This bridges the gap between recognition and the validation rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Given that Felix is trustworthy and the rule linking trustworthiness to honesty, we deduce that Felix is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(felix)</logic>
</step>

With Felix established as honest and assuming no ab_validated flag, the default validation rule applies, giving us that Felix is validated. This directly addresses the query about his validation status.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since Felix is honest and there is no abnormality preventing validation, the default rule yields that Felix is validated.</text>
<logic>validated(felix)</logic>
</step>

We derived Felix is validated, so the conclusion Felix is not validated is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the crew, Aspen, Derek, James, and Keith have all accumulated a lot of flight time, so each of them is experienced. West, on the other hand, is known for being supportive. Whenever something is experienced, it is also organized; thus Aspen’s experience makes her organized. Any organized individual is automatically industrious, so Aspen’s organization guarantees that she is industrious. If someone is energetic, then they are compassionate, which means Aspen’s potential energy would give her compassion. Aspen’s energy and her lack of industriousness cannot both be false, so either she is not industrious or she is energetic. Likewise, West’s politeness is tied to her supportiveness, and West’s vigor would make her proactive, but we also know that either West is not polite or she is vigorous. These statements capture all the facts, conditional rules, and the two disjunctive statements that we will use in the proof.
examples.question: Is it true that Aspen is not compassionate?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Aspen lacks compassion. The goal is to see if the negation of compassionate(aspen) can be upheld given the information about Aspen’s experience and the logical connections among the predicates. To answer, we will follow the chain of implications and the provided disjunction.

The first rule tells us that energetic behavior guarantees compassion for Aspen. This will be crucial later when we manage to establish Aspen’s energetic status, allowing us to derive compassion directly.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is energetic, then Aspen is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(aspen) :- energetic(aspen)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links organization to industriousness. Knowing Aspen is organized will let us infer that she is industrious, which will interact with the disjunctive premise about industriousness and energy.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is organized, then Aspen is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(aspen) :- organized(aspen)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that experience implies organization. Since we already have Aspen’s experience as a fact, this rule provides the bridge from a concrete fact to the property we need for later steps.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is experienced, then Aspen is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(aspen) :- experienced(aspen)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a logical alternative: either Aspen is not industrious or she is energetic. This will allow us to perform a disjunctive syllogism once we know the truth value of one of the options.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Aspen is not industrious or Aspen is energetic.</text>
<logic>~industrious(aspen) v energetic(aspen)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Aspen is experienced is our starting point. It is a concrete assertion that will trigger the rule connecting experience to organization.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Aspen is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(aspen)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that experienced things are organized, we can directly conclude that Aspen is organized because she is experienced. This step moves us from a known fact to a new property needed for the next implication.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From Aspen’s experience we infer that Aspen is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(aspen)</logic>
</step>

With Aspen now known to be organized, we invoke the rule that organized entities are industrious. Hence, Aspen becomes industrious, giving us the positive side of the disjunction we will later use.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Because Aspen is organized, we conclude that Aspen is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(aspen)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Aspen is industrious, which makes the first part of the disjunction (~industrious) false. Therefore, by disjunctive syllogism, the remaining alternative must be true, so Aspen is energetic.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Using the disjunction and the fact that Aspen is industrious, we deduce that Aspen is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(aspen)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that Aspen is energetic, we can finally use the first rule that energetic implies compassionate. This gives us a direct derivation that Aspen is compassionate.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Given Aspen’s energy, we infer that Aspen is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(aspen)</logic>
</step>

We derived Aspen is compassionate, so the conclusion Aspen is not compassionate is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Within the chess club, Nadia is known to be cooperative. Ari, Hector, and Oakley each demonstrate efficiency in their play, while Harley is recognized for his strength. Whenever someone is efficient, that person becomes anxious; in particular, Hector’s efficiency makes him anxious. If Hector were motivated, however, he would not be anxious. Cooperation by Nadia guarantees her creativity. Should Nadia happen to be organized, she would also be fair‑minded. Harley’s strength ensures that he is prepared, and if Harley is well‑prepared then he is logical. Moreover, either Nadia is not creative or she is organized, and either Harley is not prepared or he is well‑prepared. No default rules apply.
examples.question: Is it true that Hector is not motivated?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the claim that Hector is not motivated holds given the information about the players. The goal is to use the available facts and conditional statements to reach a conclusion about Hector's motivation.

The first rule tells us that efficiency leads to anxiety for Hector. This connection is crucial because we already know Hector's efficiency, so it provides a way to derive his emotional state. Recognizing this implication sets up the chain of reasoning that follows.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Hector is efficient, then Hector is anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(hector) :- efficient(hector)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links motivation to the negation of anxiety: if Hector were motivated, he would not be anxious. This rule will allow us to test the possibility of Hector being motivated by checking whether its consequence conflicts with what we can derive about his anxiety.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Hector is motivated, then Hector is not anxious.</text>
<logic>~anxious(hector) :- motivated(hector)</logic>
</step>

We are given directly that Hector is efficient. This fact activates the first rule, enabling us to infer something about his anxiety. It serves as the factual anchor for the deduction that follows.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Hector is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(hector)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that Hector is efficient and the rule that efficiency implies anxiety, we can conclude that Hector is anxious. This step applies straightforward forward reasoning: the premise satisfies the antecedent of the conditional, so the consequent follows.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Because Hector is efficient and the rule linking efficiency to anxiety, we can conclude that Hector is anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(hector)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Hector being anxious, while the second rule says that if Hector were motivated, he would be not anxious. Since we already know he is anxious, the condition "not anxious" cannot hold, which means the antecedent (motivated) must be false. Therefore, we deduce that Hector is not motivated.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since Hector is anxious and the rule says that being motivated would make him not anxious, we infer that Hector cannot be motivated; therefore, Hector is not motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(hector)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hector is not motivated, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the library setting the following facts are known: Jordan has the property ab_brave. Cora is courageous. Ari is talented. Jordan is also talented. The rule for Ari says that if Ari is brave then Ari is adaptable. Whenever Cora is energetic, Cora is optimistic. If Cora is courageous, then Cora is patient. Either Cora is not patient or Cora is energetic. A default rule states that if someone is talented and there is no evidence that they are not brave, then they are brave; applied to Ari this means that because Ari is talented and we have no fact ab_brave(ari), Ari can be considered brave. Another default rule says that if someone is adaptable and there is no evidence that they are not fit, then they are fit; applied to Ari this means that if Ari becomes adaptable and we lack ab_fit(ari), Ari will be regarded as fit.
examples.question: Is Ari fit according to the information given?

This premise connects bravery to adaptability for Ari, which will be useful later once we can establish Ari’s bravery. It tells us that proving Ari is brave is enough to obtain Ari’s adaptability.

<logic>adaptable(ari) :- brave(ari)</logic>

The default rule gives us a way to derive bravery from talent, provided we lack contrary evidence about Ari’s bravery. Since we know Ari is talented and have no statement that Ari is not brave, this rule becomes applicable.
<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>brave(ari) :- talented(ari), not ab_brave(ari)</logic>
</step>

This default rule will let us turn adaptability into fitness, again assuming we have no evidence that Ari is not fit. It sets up the final step of the reasoning once we have shown Ari is adaptable.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>A default rule says that if Ari is adaptable and there is no evidence that Ari is not fit, then Ari is regarded as fit.</text>
<logic>fit(ari) :- adaptable(ari), not ab_fit(ari)</logic>
</step>

Ari being talented is a concrete fact that serves as the starting point for the default rule about bravery. Without this fact we could not trigger the default that yields bravery.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Ari is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(ari)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that Ari is talented with the default rule that talent (and no contrary evidence) leads to bravery, we can infer Ari is brave. The absence of any ab_brave(ari) statement satisfies the ‘not ab_brave’ condition, allowing the default to fire.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From Ari’s talent and the default rule about bravery, we infer that Ari is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(ari)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Ari is brave, we can apply the earlier rule that brave implies adaptable. This is a straightforward modus ponens step: the antecedent (brave) holds, so the consequent (adaptable) follows.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Ari is brave, the earlier rule lets us conclude that Ari is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(ari)</logic>
</step>

Having derived Ari’s adaptability, we turn to the default rule about fitness. Because we have no information that Ari is not fit, the condition ‘not ab_fit’ is satisfied, so the default yields that Ari is fit.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>With Ari being adaptable, the default rule about fitness allows us to deduce that Ari is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(ari)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Ari is fit, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Lane is alert. Keith is resourceful. Cameron is thorough. Whenever someone is alert, that person is focused; thus Lane being alert means Lane is focused. If someone is thorough, then that person is goal-oriented; therefore Cameron being thorough makes Cameron goal-oriented. If someone is empathetic, then they are insightful; thus any empathetic person would be insightful, and in particular if Cameron were empathetic, he would be insightful. If someone is brave, then they are respectful; so a brave Lane would be respectful. If someone is trustworthy, then they are not resourceful; thus a trustworthy Keith would not be resourceful. If someone is optimistic, then they are evidence-driven; therefore an optimistic Keith would be evidence-driven. Additionally, it is known that Keith is either trustworthy or optimistic. Also, either Lane is not focused or Lane is brave. Finally, either Cameron is not goal-oriented or Cameron is empathetic.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude that Keith is evidence-driven?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Keith possesses the property of being evidence-driven. The goal is to use the supplied facts, rules, and disjunctions to derive this conclusion step by step.

The rule linking optimism to evidence‑driven behavior tells us that optimism is sufficient for being evidence‑driven. This connection will be crucial once we establish Keith's optimism.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Keith is optimistic, then Keith is evidence-driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(keith) :- optimistic(keith)</logic>
</step>

The rule about trustworthiness says that a trustworthy individual cannot be resourceful. This will allow us to eliminate trustworthiness if we can show Keith is indeed resourceful.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Keith is trustworthy, then Keith is not resourceful.</text>
<logic>~resourceful(keith) :- trustworthy(keith)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice: Keith is either trustworthy or optimistic. By narrowing down one side, we can force the other side to hold.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Keith is trustworthy or Keith is optimistic.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(keith) v optimistic(keith)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Keith is resourceful is directly provided in the context and will interact with the trustworthiness rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Keith is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(keith)</logic>
</step>

Because the trustworthiness rule states that a trustworthy person would not be resourceful, but we know Keith is resourceful, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Keith is not trustworthy. This is an application of modus tollens: if P implies not Q and Q is true, then P must be false.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since a trustworthy person would not be resourceful and Keith actually is resourceful, we infer that Keith cannot be trustworthy.</text>
<logic>~trustworthy(keith)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Keith is not trustworthy, the earlier disjunction (trustworthy or optimistic) cannot be satisfied by the trustworthy option. Consequently, the optimistic alternative must be true, giving us Keith's optimism. This follows the principle of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Given that Keith is not trustworthy and that either he is trustworthy or optimistic, we conclude that Keith must be optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(keith)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Keith's optimism, the first rule applies: optimism guarantees that the individual is evidence‑driven. By modus ponens, from optimism we infer that Keith is evidence‑driven.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Because Keith is optimistic and optimism guarantees being evidence-driven, we deduce that Keith is evidence-driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(keith)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Keith is evidence driven, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In our study of marine life, the sea turtle is known to be a research subject. Whenever the sea turtle is deep-diving, it is also migratory. If the sea turtle is migratory, then it cannot be a research subject. No other facts or defaults are provided.
examples.question: Is the sea turtle deep-diving?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the sea turtle exhibits deep-diving behavior. To answer this, we must combine the given facts and conditional statements about its properties.

The first rule tells us that deep-diving implies migratory for the sea turtle. This connection will be useful later when we try to reason about deep-diving from information about migration.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the sea turtle is deep-diving, then it is migratory.</text>
<logic>migratory(sea_turtle) :- deep_diving(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that being migratory forces the sea turtle not to be a research subject. This creates a link between migration and the negation of the known fact, allowing us to apply contraposition.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the sea turtle is migratory, then it is not a research subject.</text>
<logic>~research_subject(sea_turtle) :- migratory(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The sea turtle is a research subject.</text>
<logic>research_subject(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

We know the sea turtle is a research subject. The rule from premise 2 says that if it were migratory, it would not be a research subject. By modus tollens, the truth of the research‑subject fact forces the negation of the antecedent, so the sea turtle cannot be migratory.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because the sea turtle is a research subject, and if it were migratory it would not be a research subject, we conclude the sea turtle is not migratory.</text>
<logic>~migratory(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the sea turtle is not migratory, we return to premise 1, which says deep-diving would make it migratory. Again using modus tollens, the falsity of the migratory conclusion lets us infer that the sea turtle is not deep-diving.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since the sea turtle is not migratory, and if it were deep-diving it would be migratory, we infer the sea turtle is not deep-diving.</text>
<logic>~deep_diving(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

We derived Sea_turtle is not deep diving, so the conclusion Sea_turtle is deep diving is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In this ancient setting, Wren is motivated, Kit is punctual, and Olive is scholarly. Whenever something is punctual, it is also analytical, so Kit being punctual makes Kit analytical. If something is scholarly, then it is articulate, which means Olive being scholarly guarantees Olive is articulate. Being athletic ensures one is content, so any athletic entity would be content. Motivation leads to experience, therefore a motivated individual becomes experienced; thus Wren being motivated implies Wren is experienced. Proactivity brings inventiveness, so if someone is proactive they become inventive, though we have no fact about Wren's proactivity. Additionally, we know that either Kit is not analytical or Kit is methodical, and either Olive is not articulate or Olive is athletic, and either Wren is not experienced or Wren is proactive.
examples.question: Is it true that Kit is not methodical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Kit lacks the property of being methodical. To answer, we must examine the information that connects Kit's punctuality to analyticity and the logical alternatives that involve methodicalness. By following the given rules and facts, we can see if the negation of methodical can hold.

The first premise provides a conditional link: punctuality guarantees analyticity. This is directly relevant because we already know Kit is punctual, so the rule gives us a way to obtain analyticity for Kit, which will be needed later in the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Kit is punctual, then Kit is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(kit) :- punctual(kit)</logic>
</step>

The second premise offers a disjunction that sets up two exclusive possibilities concerning Kit: either Kit is not analytical or Kit is methodical. This statement will allow us to use a disjunctive syllogism once we have information about Kit's analyticity.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>We have a disjunction saying that either Kit is not analytical or Kit is methodical.</text>
<logic>~analytical(kit) v methodical(kit)</logic>
</step>

The third premise is a straightforward fact that Kit is punctual. This fact activates the conditional from PREMISE_1, enabling us to derive a new property for Kit.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that Kit is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(kit)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, we combine Kit's punctuality (from PREMISE_3) with the rule that punctuality implies analyticity (from PREMISE_1). This yields the conclusion that Kit is analytical, a crucial piece for resolving the disjunction in PREMISE_2.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From Kit being punctual and the rule linking punctuality to analyticity, we infer that Kit is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(kit)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Kit is analytical, the first part of the disjunction (~analytical(kit)) is false. By the principle of disjunctive syllogism, the remaining alternative must be true, so Kit is methodical. This directly contradicts the query that Kit is not methodical.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Using the disjunction and the newly derived fact that Kit is analytical, we conclude that Kit must be methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(kit)</logic>
</step>

We derived Kit is methodical, so the conclusion Kit is not methodical is False.
</think>
Answer: False

Whenever someone is ranked they become competitive, thus Nora is competitive. Additionally, either Emerson is not clear-headed or Emerson is confident, and either Olive is not perceptive or Olive is athletic. Finally, if someone is athletic, they are respectful, so any athletic Olive would be respectful. The default rule states that a competitive Nora who lacks any abnormality (ab_diligent) is diligent.
examples.question: Is Nora diligent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a small mythic world where several attributes of characters are linked by conditional statements. Our goal is to determine whether Nora can be concluded to be diligent based on the provided facts and rules.

The rule that a ranked individual becomes competitive is important because it connects the status of being ranked to the property we need later. Recognizing this link will let us move from a ranking fact to competitiveness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Nora is ranked, then she is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(nora) :- ranked(nora)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that efficiency guarantees ranking. This bridge is crucial because we already know Nora is efficient, so we can obtain the ranking needed for the next step. It sets up the chain of inference.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Nora is efficient, then she is ranked.</text>
<logic>ranked(nora) :- efficient(nora)</logic>
</step>

The default rule states that any competitive Nora without an abnormality will be diligent. This is the final piece that will allow us to derive diligence once we have established competitiveness. It also mentions the absence of an abnormality, which we assume holds unless contradicted.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Nora is competitive and there is no abnormality preventing diligence, then Nora is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(nora) :- competitive(nora), not ab_diligent(nora)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that Nora is efficient. This fact is the starting point of our forward reasoning. It will trigger the rule that turns efficiency into a ranking.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Nora is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(nora)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Nora is efficient together with the rule that efficiency implies ranking, we can assert that Nora is ranked. This application of modus ponens moves us from a known fact to a new property, setting the stage for the next inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since Nora is efficient, we can infer that she is ranked.</text>
<logic>ranked(nora)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Nora is ranked, we apply the rule that a ranked individual is competitive. By again using modus ponens, we derive that Nora is competitive, which is a necessary condition for the default rule about diligence.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Because Nora is ranked, we conclude that she is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(nora)</logic>
</step>

With Nora's competitiveness confirmed and no information indicating an abnormality, the default rule activates, allowing us to conclude that Nora is diligent. The default reasoning respects the lack of a blocking abnormality and yields the desired property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given that Nora is competitive and there is no abnormality, we infer that Nora is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(nora)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Nora is diligent, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Morgan has accumulated a great deal of experience in fashion design. Whenever someone is experienced, they are also principled, so Morgan is principled. Any principled person is empathetic, which makes Morgan empathetic. Being empathetic guarantees calmness, therefore Morgan is calm. If someone is polite, they are impartial, and any impartial person is also polite; thus impartiality and politeness are linked for Morgan. Politeness leads to loyalty, so if Morgan is polite then she is loyal. Loyalty, in turn, brings cheerfulness, meaning a loyal person is cheerful. Respectful behavior prevents calmness: if someone is respectful then they are not calm. Finally, either Morgan is respectful or she is impartial. All of these statements together constitute the knowledge base.
examples.question: Is it true that Morgan is not cheerful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Morgan is not cheerful based on a network of personality‑style rules. The goal is to see if the premises inevitably lead to Morgan being cheerful, which would make the negation false.

The first rule links empathy to calmness; recognizing this connection will help us trace a path from experience to calm later on. It is a direct conditional that we will later apply once we know Morgan is empathetic.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Morgan is empathetic, then Morgan is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(morgan) :- empathetic(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that loyalty guarantees cheerfulness. This is crucial because if we can derive loyalty for Morgan, we will automatically obtain cheerfulness, which directly contradicts the query.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Morgan is loyal, then Morgan is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(morgan) :- loyal(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that being principled leads to empathy. Since we already have a fact about Morgan's experience, this rule will allow us to move from experience to empathy, and then via PREMISE_1 to calmness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Morgan is principled, then Morgan is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(morgan) :- principled(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says politeness yields loyalty. Once we have politeness, we can climb the chain toward loyalty and ultimately cheerfulness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Morgan is polite, then Morgan is loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(morgan) :- polite(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule creates a bridge from impartiality to politeness. This will become relevant after we use the disjunction to establish that Morgan is impartial.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Morgan is impartial, then Morgan is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(morgan) :- impartial(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule connects experience with being principled. Because we know Morgan is experienced, this rule immediately gives us a principled status, setting off the chain of implications.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Morgan is experienced, then Morgan is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(morgan) :- experienced(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The seventh rule is a negative conditional: if Morgan were respectful, she could not be calm. This will be useful later to eliminate the respectful alternative from the disjunction.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Morgan is respectful, then Morgan is not calm.</text>
<logic>~calm(morgan) :- respectful(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The eighth premise provides a choice: Morgan is either respectful or impartial. By showing that the respectful option is impossible, we will be forced to accept the impartial alternative.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Morgan is respectful or Morgan is impartial.</text>
<logic>respectful(morgan) v impartial(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The factual premise asserts that Morgan is experienced, the starting point for several forward inferences.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Morgan is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(morgan)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the fact that Morgan is experienced and the rule that experience implies principled, we directly infer that Morgan is principled. This step transforms the raw fact into a more useful property for later steps.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>From Morgan being experienced and the rule that experience implies principled, we infer that Morgan is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(morgan)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have principled, we use the rule that principled leads to empathy. Modus ponens again gives us empathy, moving us one link further along the chain toward calmness.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Since Morgan is principled, the rule that principled implies empathetic lets us conclude that Morgan is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(morgan)</logic>
</step>

With empathy established, the rule linking empathy to calmness lets us derive that Morgan is calm. This calmness will later clash with the possibility of respectfulness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Having established that Morgan is empathetic, the rule that empathetic implies calm yields that Morgan is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(morgan)</logic>
</step>

Because we now know Morgan is calm, the rule that respectful would make her not calm forces a contradiction if she were respectful. By modus tollens, we conclude Morgan cannot be respectful, i.e., she is not respectful.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Because Morgan is calm, the rule that respectful would make her not calm forces us to deduce that Morgan is not respectful.</text>
<logic>~respectful(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says Morgan is either respectful or impartial. Since we have just shown she cannot be respectful, the only remaining option is that she is impartial. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>With Morgan not respectful and the disjunction that she is either respectful or impartial, we must accept that she is impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(morgan)</logic>
</step>

Impartiality triggers the rule that impartial implies polite. Using modus ponens, we infer that Morgan is polite, bringing us closer to loyalty.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Impartiality triggers the rule that impartial implies polite, so Morgan is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(morgan)</logic>
</step>

Politeness then activates the rule that polite implies loyal. Applying modus ponens again, we obtain loyalty for Morgan, a pivotal property for cheerfulness.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,4">
<text>Politeness activates the rule that polite implies loyal, giving us that Morgan is loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(morgan)</logic>
</step>

Finally, loyalty activates the rule that loyal implies cheerful. Modus ponens gives us that Morgan is cheerful, directly opposing the query's claim.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,2">
<text>Loyalty then activates the rule that loyal implies cheerful, so Morgan is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(morgan)</logic>
</step>

We derived Morgan is cheerful, so the conclusion Morgan is not cheerful is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is it true that Lara is not flexible?

We are asked whether Lara lacks flexibility. The premises give a chain of conditionals linking stress, equipment, resolve, satisfaction, skill, goal orientation, steadiness, and finally flexibility. Our goal is to see if the opposite of flexibility can be derived from the given information.

If Lara were skilled, she would be satisfied. This rule will be used in a contrapositive fashion to infer the absence of skill from the absence of satisfaction.

Being equipped guarantees resoluteness. Once we obtain equipment, we can immediately claim Lara is resolute.

A satisfied Lara cannot be resolute. This creates a tension between satisfaction and resoluteness that we will exploit via contrapositive reasoning.

From the fact that Lara is stressed and the rule that stress implies equipment, we conclude Lara is equipped. This follows directly by applying the conditional in a forward direction.

<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">

The rule connecting skill to satisfaction tells us that if Lara were skilled, she would be satisfied. Because we have just derived that she is not satisfied, the contrapositive tells us that Lara cannot be skilled.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">

examples.context: Network admin Sarah is known for being adaptive. Whenever the admin is competent, she is also stable. Being adaptive guarantees that the admin is reliable. If the admin is persistent, then she is not stable. Either the admin is not reliable, or she is competent.
examples.question: Is Sarah persistent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the network admin Sarah has the property of being persistent. The query asks for patient(sarah), which in our mapping corresponds to "persistent". Our goal is to see if the premises force this property to be true or false. By examining the given facts and rules we can build a chain of implications that will settle the question.

The first rule links competence to stability: any admin who is competent is also stable. This will be useful later when we obtain competence for Sarah, allowing us to derive stability. Keeping this conditional in mind sets up a forward direction for the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Sarah is competent, then she is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(network_admin) :- competent(network_admin)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects adaptiveness to reliability. Since we already know Sarah is adaptive, this rule will let us infer reliability, which will later interact with the disjunction about reliability and competence. It establishes a clear path from a known fact to a new property.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Sarah is adaptive, then she is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(network_admin) :- adaptive(network_admin)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that persistence would contradict stability: if Sarah were persistent, she would not be stable. This contrapositive relationship will let us turn a derived stability into a denial of persistence later on. It is the key to turning a positive fact into a negative conclusion about persistence.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Sarah is persistent, then she is not stable.</text>
<logic>~stable(network_admin) :- persistent(network_admin)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of two possibilities holds: Sarah is either not reliable or she is competent. This ambiguous statement will become decisive once we know whether she is reliable; if she is, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is for her to be competent. Thus the disjunction will help us obtain competence.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The statement says that either Sarah is not reliable or Sarah is competent.</text>
<logic>~reliable(network_admin) v competent(network_admin)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Sarah is adaptive is our starting point. It directly satisfies the antecedent of the reliability rule, so we can immediately apply that rule. This concrete fact anchors the entire reasoning chain.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Sarah is adaptive.</text>
<logic>adaptive(network_admin)</logic>
</step>

Applying the reliability rule to the fact that Sarah is adaptive yields that Sarah is reliable. This step is a straightforward use of modus ponens: the antecedent (adaptive) is true, so the consequent (reliable) follows. Now we have a concrete truth about reliability that will interact with the disjunction.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Sarah being adaptive and the rule that adaptive implies reliable, we conclude that Sarah is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(network_admin)</logic>
</step>

We now have Sarah being reliable, and the disjunction tells us that either she is not reliable or she is competent. Since the first option is false (she is reliable), the disjunction can only be satisfied by the second option. Hence we infer that Sarah must be competent. This is an instance of disjunctive syllogism, eliminating the impossible disjunct.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Using Sarah being reliable together with the disjunction that either she is not reliable or she is competent, we infer that Sarah must be competent.</text>
<logic>competent(network_admin)</logic>
</step>

With competence established, we turn to the first rule, which says competence leads to stability. By modus ponens again, because Sarah is competent, we can conclude that she is stable. This gives us a positive fact that will later be used to contradict persistence.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Sarah is competent, the rule that competence leads to stability lets us infer that Sarah is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(network_admin)</logic>
</step>

We now possess the fact that Sarah is stable, and we also have the rule that persistence would make her not stable. Using modus tollens, if persistence implied not stable, but we observe stability, the only consistent conclusion is that Sarah is not persistent. Thus we derive the negation of the queried property.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Because Sarah is stable and the rule that persistence would make her not stable, we deduce that Sarah cannot be persistent.</text>
<logic>~persistent(network_admin)</logic>
</step>

We derived Network_admin is not persistent, so the conclusion Network_admin is persistent is False.
</think>
Answer: False

Given that Carl is diligent, the first option of the disjunction (~diligent(carl)) is false, so the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second option, active(carl), to be true. Thus we can safely infer that Carl is active.

Now that we have established Carl's activity, we can apply the rule from PREMISE_1: an active Carl must be brave. This straightforward application of modus ponens gives us the conclusion that Carl is brave.

With Carl's bravery confirmed, we turn to PREMISE_2, which tells us that any brave Carl is reflective. By another direct application of modus ponens, we conclude that Carl is indeed reflective.

Therefore Carl is reflective, so the conclusion is True.
